articles.title: Transfiguring Smithian Liberalism: The Intellectual Legacy of Hiroshi Mizuta and Hideo Tanaka in Japan
articles.abstract: Historians have noted how Smith’s name has been invoked to justify liberal economic policies in diverse national contexts. The historical elasticity and political implications of Smithian economic liberalism become clearer by examining the case of Japan. The previous studies of the reception of Smith in Japan have not sufficiently explored its liberal implications after the postwar period. Attention to these implications is essential for understanding not only the popularity of Smith studies in Japan but also the rapid growth of research on the Scottish Enlightenment from the 1970s onward, a period when the field remained comparatively small outside Britain. These two distinctive features of Japanese scholarship are closely connected, yet their relationship has not been systematically analyzed. This paper argues that this connection becomes clearer when examined through the intellectual trajectories of Hiroshi Mizuta and Hideo Tanaka, two influential scholars on the studies of Smith and the Scottish Enlightenment.

articles.title: Thomas Edward Cliffe Leslie and the political economy of British land systems: From the critique of the classical landed property reform to the birth of Irish historical economics
articles.abstract: Thomas Edward Cliffe Leslie is an influential figure in the Irish history of economic thought. This paper presents Leslie’s critical appraisal of British land systems and discusses his critique towards the classical landed property reform. The paper illustrates the affinities between Leslie’s and John Stuart Mill's economic thought regarding the Irish land question. Furthermore, it shows that Leslie’s support for small proprietorship in Ireland is regarded as a prerequisite for defending the Act of Union of 1800. Lastly, the paper argues that Leslie’s political economy of British land systems is a significant stream in the foundation of the Irish historical school and in the promotion of an alternative political economy.

articles.title: Concentrated Affluence: The Spatial Segregation of High-Income Households across Richmond, VA
articles.abstract: Researchers examining issues of inequality focus overwhelmingly on racially segregated neighborhoods, concentrated poverty, and spatial isolation. Such focus is appropriate, given that spatial concentration amplifies the harms of poverty. But this overemphasis has driven policymakers to prioritize mitigating concentration, as evidenced by numerous dispersal policies. In contrast, scholars pay far less attention to the opposite extreme: the concentration of affluent households. The spatial concentration of affluence enables advantaged households to hoard opportunity, skew resource distribution, limit access to public amenities, and consolidate political power. Across public discourse, concentrated affluence is routinely treated as neutral, and policymakers rarely pursue strategies to confront this consequential component of segregation. This study focuses on the segregation of affluence, examining changes in the concentration of high-income households since 1990. Using Richmond, Virginia, as a case study, findings demonstrate that affluent households are the most unevenly distributed group, rapidly segregating in the city and growing more concentrated in the suburbs. This paper makes two contributions: First, it captures changes in the geography of poverty and affluence by tracking low- and high-income households. Second, it provides an intraurban analysis of segregation, detailing household sorting across urban and suburban spaces. Growing spatial segregation of affluent households reveals a planning system that privileges the advantaged, and a focus on concentrated affluence is essential for urban policy to address spatial inequality.

articles.title: Policing the Screen, Screening the Policed: Immigrant Representation, Surveillance, and Racial Capitalism in Icelandic Cinema
articles.abstract: Abstract: This article develops an anthropological reading of immigrant representation in Icelandic cinema through the lens of racial capitalism. Focusing on Ísold Uggadóttir's Andið eðlilega / And Breathe Normally (2018), alongside Tryggð / The Deposit (2019), Gullregn (2020), Kona fer í stríð / Woman at War (2018), Ikíngut (2000), Ófærð / Trapped (2015–), and a wider corpus of films and series, it argues that recent Icelandic audio-visual production renders visible the surveillance and policing infrastructures through which racialised mobility is governed at the Nordic periphery. The film analysis is brought into dialogue with empirical research on police–minority relations in Iceland, which documents perceptions of bias and procedural injustice among persons of colour and migrants, and with scholarship on whiteness, Nordic exceptionalism, and crimmigration in the wider region. Drawing on a small but theoretically informed film corpus and a typology of immigrant subjectivities, the article shows how cinematic framings of asylum seekers, Indigenous children, low-budget tourists and migrant workers both reproduce and contest Icelandic narratives of innocence. The article concludes with policy implications for anti-racist reform of Icelandic border and policing practices, and for public support of migrant-centred film-making.

subjects.name: Social and Philosophical Foundations of Education

articles.title: From Fire to Algorithms: Why Fear of AI Is Not New, But Still Matters
articles.abstract: Eight in ten researchers already use AI tools in their work. Most will not say so out loud in a conference room. This paper examines that gap, the space between what academics do privately and what they defend publicly and argues that it is costing the research community something real. Using documented adoption data across medicine, education, coding, and creative industries, alongside historical cases from the introduction of writing to the Industrial Revolution, the paper makes the case that the fear surrounding AI in academia is not new, but the silence around it is a choice. And it is a choice with consequences. Keywords: Artificial Intelligence, Academic Culture, Research Methodology, AI Adoption, Higher Education, Technology Adoption.

articles.title: The Second-Generation Boom: U.S.-born Children of Immigrants’ Growing Contribution to Working-Age Demographics, Socioeconomics, and Health
articles.abstract: Since the 1965 Immigration and Nationality Act, an increased number of foreign-born individuals have been renewing the U.S. labor force and countering population aging. While immigration is currently slowing down, the second-generation population is reaching prime working age in growing numbers. In this study, we use the Current Population Survey to document demographic trends from 2000-25 and quantify the impact of the second-generation on the labor force. We show that the second-generation population aged 25-64 has doubled since the turn of the century, accounting for more than half of the labor force growth in three out of five largest states. The median age of the population has declined by five years over this period, even as it has increased for the first- and third+ generations. Although we observe many subgroup differences that echo well-documented patterns of stratification within immigrants, we also show that the working-age second-generation are overall demonstrating strong socioeconomic and health outcomes. Together with their rising population share, the second-generation has already played an important role in countering a decline in the overall labor force participation rate and driving improvement in population health.

articles.title: Philosophy as Cognitive Assay: Measuring the Delegation Legitimacy Boundary in AI-Assisted Knowledge Work
articles.abstract: This article operationalizes the concept of a delegation legitimacy boundary — the structural line along which human judgment can and cannot be legitimately delegated to artificial intelligence — and proposes a minimal scoring protocol for locating it within any knowledge-work task. Building on the Mekiki framework (Tomita 2026), which distinguished specification — the task-defining substrate of domain expertise — from externalization cost — the technical barrier that AI selectively removes — the present article derives a further decomposition within specification itself. Drawing on case-study evidence in which recorded specification judgments contain separable factual components (Sein-type: what the data structure affords, how users will perceive a display) and value-laden components (Sollen-type: what information ought to be excluded, which priority ranking is appropriate), the article grounds this distinction in Hume’s is–ought separation and Kant’s Sein/Sollen architecture, and redeploys it as the axis of a cognitive assay — a measurement system in which philosophical categories serve not as normative prescriptions but as diagnostic coordinates. The resulting five-step scoring protocol assigns Sein-type components to AI evaluation and Sollen-type components to domain-expert evaluation; this asymmetry is not a design choice but a structural consequence of the boundary itself, which holds as long as legitimacy over value judgments remains institutionally human-attributed. Most individual judgments are hybrid, carrying both components in varying ratios; the protocol therefore yields ratio profiles rather than binary classifications. As a first application, the article re-describes the four processes of Nonaka and Takeuchi’s Socialization–Externalization–Combination–Internalization (SECI) model through the assay, deriving as an analytic consequence the finding that AI acceleration of Externalization and Combination shifts the effective rate-limiting stage to Socialization and Internalization — both human-limited cognitive and social processes that cannot be accelerated by AI investment alone.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Execution‑Time Authority in AI‑Enabled Administration: A Structural Model for Public Decision Systems
articles.abstract: Public administrations increasingly deploy AI‑enabled systems to execute statutory functions. Yet existing regulatory frameworks lack conceptual tools to identify where public authority is exercised within these systems. This paper develops a unified governance architecture that traces how authority originates in legislated purpose, becomes actionable through delegation, and is operationalised through embedded decision structures.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Layer-Skipping Disclosure in Ritualized Containers: Sanctioned Shadow Surfacing as a Mechanism Beyond Social Penetration Theory
articles.abstract: Social penetration theory (Altman & Taylor, 1973) holds that self-disclosure proceeds gradually from outer to inner layers, with rapid disclosure typically explained by anonymity or reduced future accountability. We identify a theoretically anomalous phenomenon current frameworks cannot accommodate: layer-skipping disclosure in fully non-anonymous, sustained-relationship contexts. This occurs among high-functioning socialized individuals—those who maintain a strategic, self-aware adapted false self as social competence rather than reactive defense. We propose Sanctioned Shadow Surfacing (SSS) as a mechanism-level account of how ritualized social fields make masked self-state content discloseable under accountable relational conditions. Here this content takes the form of self-aware but routinely masked shadow aspects ('shadow' used descriptively rather than metaphysically). Ritualized subcultural containers operate as dual-permission structures: the field grants specific invitation to self-state surfacing while the individual actively elects to un-mask. The driving force is recognition-by-peer (the discloser's confidence that the witness possesses interpretive competence to receive the content intelligibly) rather than reciprocity or reduced social cost. We illustrate through two cases along an intensity spectrum: BDSM scenes and Monsterhearts 2 tabletop role-play. We distinguish disclosure from integration, clarifying what such containers can and cannot deliver. [Note: This paper is currently under peer review.]

articles.title: The Insecurity Triad (Part 1): Foundations of Convergence and Rival Sovereignty — An Analysis of Money, Land, and Mind (MLM)
articles.abstract: In this landmark inaugural paper, Max Amuchie, architect of The Insecurity Triad and the Trinity of State Decay theory, introduces the MLM Framework (Money, Land, and Mind) as a definitive analytical architecture for diagnosing the structural collapse of state sovereignty. Published as part of the Sundiata Post Intelligence Unit (SPIU) Working Paper Series, the study posits that kidnapping, banditry, and terrorism function as a synergistic "Insecurity Triad"—a convergence of mutually constitutive forces. This research contests conventional theories of "state failure," arguing instead that these forces produce a sophisticated structural contest over legitimate authority. By applying the MLM Framework, the author demonstrates how non-state actors do not merely create chaos; they establish a "Rival Order" by seizing control of illicit economic arteries (Money), territorial sovereignty (Land), and the psychological loyalty of the citizenry (Mind). Drawing upon a rigorous theoretical genealogy—bridging the seminal insights of Ali Mazrui, Claude Ake, Achille Mbembe, Jean-François Bayart, and William Reno—the author situates this framework within the vanguard of African political thought while asserting its status as an original, predictive doctrine. While Nigeria serves as the generative case study, the framework’s logic is universal: it travels wherever these three forces coalesce to replace institutional governance with shadow sovereignty. The paper argues that contemporary security discourse is fundamentally flawed, as it remains tethered to isolated threat categories that fail to perceive the convergent logic of modern insecurity. THE INSECURITY TRIAD provides the necessary corrective, offering an integrated strategic architecture that positions state recovery not as the repair of a "failed" entity, but as the deliberate production of a new political and security equilibrium.

articles.title: AI-Powered CBT Chatbots for Young Adults: A Systematic Review of Efficacy, Engagement, and Ethical Concerns
articles.abstract: Background: AI-powered CBT chatbots are a promising way to scale mental health interventions based on demand, eliminating hurdles such as cost and social stigma. Young adults are an especially vulnerable subset of the population, who could potentially benefit from these digital mental health interventions. Objective: This systematic literature review aimed to determine the factors of AI-powered CBT chatbots that promote or impede young adult engagement, as well as the effectiveness and ethical considerations. Methods: In this review, 24 papers were systematically screened from PubMed (primary) and Google Scholar within the timeline 2015 to 2026. Papers studying CBT chatbots and population consisting of 18 to 39 years were included in this review. Results: AI-powered CBT chatbots have a small to moderate effect in alleviating symptoms of depression, anxiety and hopelessness. Quality engagement improves the effectiveness of these chatbots, however, sustained adherence is an issue. Users engage mainly due to need, followed by anonymity, non-judgemental nature, human-like support and round the clock availability. Overall, these digital interventions are promising but have ethical concerns such as inadequacy in risk identification, algorithmic bias, transparency issues and risk of AI psychosis. Conclusion: Overall, AI-powered CBT chatbots demonstrate potential for scalability of mental health interventions to young adults (18 to 39 years). Apart from some technological and trust related ethical concerns, these digital interventions have proven to be engaging, as well as effective on a short-term basis.

articles.title: Participatory Descriptive Phenomenology: a methodological framework
articles.authors: Mark Adley, stephanie scott, Amy O'Donnell
articles.abstract: This methodological paper presents a participatory adaptation of descriptive phenomenology, co-produced with public contributors. Traditionally, descriptive phenomenological analysis is performed by a sole trained analyst responsible for conducting bracketing, reduction, and essence-seeking. In contrast, we explore how these processes can be shared within collaborative partnerships. Through iterative development and reflexive discussion, we adapted key phenomenological processes to make them usable and meaningful for public contributors. In doing so, we reoriented the epistemic assumptions of phenomenological inquiry, with meaning-making situated within relational, participatory processes. Public contributors are seldom involved in data analysis, or when they are, are asked to assess themes already generated by researchers. We argue that a participatory descriptive phenomenology offers a promising model for inclusive, culturally grounded qualitative research. Our methodological reflections contribute to debates on power, co-production, and epistemic justice, and demonstrate how descriptive phenomenology can be expanded methodologically and theoretically to bring people’s lived experiences to the fore.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: The reasons and aims for listening to music while going to sleep: results from an ESM study
articles.authors: Rory Kirk, William Randall, Suvi Saarikallio
articles.abstract: Current trends in music research are emphasising the importance of contextual factors in the use and effects of listening to music, such as mood, situation, and emotional goals. This undoubtedly has implications for our understanding of the use of music for wellness strategies, such as helping going to sleep. Using data collected in previous experience sampling studies, we conducted an exploratory investigation on listener behaviours in the context of going to sleep with accounts reported in real-life situations. Compared to other listening activities, we found that listeners going to sleep mainly used music to relax, which corresponded with increased valence, varying arousal changes, and diverse music choices. This novel perspective echoes previously reported discrepancies in what listeners use and what researchers or commercial applications typically assume when it comes to listening to music for sleep. The data add granular information on affective aims and development in context, and particularly highlight the relevance of valence. This initial exploration demonstrates the strong potential for experience sampling methodology to study sleep and listening behaviours.

articles.title: Beyond the Takeover: Youth Gatherings, Urban Public Space, and the Racialization of Disorder in Chicago
articles.abstract: In recent years, Chicago has witnessed a significant rise in large-scale youth gatherings in high-visibility public spaces. Labeled "teen takeovers" in dominant media and policy discourse, these events are almost exclusively framed as public safety threats requiring law enforcement response. This paper argues that this framing is both analytically insufficient and politically consequential. Drawing on preliminary media and news analysis, an emerging qualitative research agenda, and a multi-disciplinary theoretical framework, this paper reframes youth gatherings as social phenomena that reveal fundamental dynamics of race, space, territorial boundary, and urban belonging. We identify three dimensions that existing scholarship has not yet systematically examined: (1) the role of social media and digital technology in organizing, escalating, and documenting these events; (2) the apparent dissolution of hyperlocal territorial boundaries historically maintained by street organizations in Chicago; and (3) the variation in form, activity, and framing of analogous youth gatherings across U.S. cities. We situate these dimensions within a five-part theoretical architecture drawing on moral panic theory, critical criminology, spatial theory, Black cultural studies, and the sociology of punishment. We conclude by presenting a research agenda for empirical investigation and discussing the implications of this work for policy and community response. Keywords: youth gatherings, teen takeovers, urban public space, moral panic, territorial boundaries, Chicago, racialization, social media, cultural criminology

articles.title: Risk Aversion with Nothing to Lose
articles.abstract: In a continuous-time model, a risk-neutral decision-maker chooses the volatility of a state variable and is terminated when the variable falls below a threshold. I provide economically interpretable conditions under which the decision-maker becomes risk averse endogenously and minimizes volatility near termination, even if she faces myopic incentives to gamble for resurrection. The conditions introduce forward-looking incentives to preserve economic rents. I show these conditions are met in a wide range of apparently unrelated models, thus identifying forward-looking rents as a unifying economic mechanism behind endogenous risk aversion. I also provide conditions for the decision-maker to become risk loving endogenously.

articles.title: Cultural Homecoming Without Return: Elephant Symbolism, Hakka Migration, and the Ritual Regeneration of Lost Homeland
articles.abstract: This paper examines how the Hakka people of southern Jiangxi, China, sustain a three-thousand-year cultural memory of Shang Dynasty elephant domestication through the "Nine Lions Worship Elephant" lantern festival. Drawing on archaeological evidence, paleographic analysis, historical geography, climate history, and ethnographic observation, I trace a symbol transmission chain from the Central Plains "Elephant Land" to a ritualized homecoming ceremony performed in villages that have never hosted live elephants. I argue that the elephant lantern functions as a "mnemonic vessel," a material cultural form that preserves and reactivates homeland identity by embodying an absent ecological referent, extending Connerton's inscribed and incorporated memory framework. The concept is offered as a theoretical extension, not a revolution, aimed at illuminating how displaced communities perform "ecological memory without ecological presence." The paper contributes to the anthropology of cultural memory, diaspora, and multispecies relations by showing that homeland can be sustained not as a territory but as a ritual technology.

articles.title: Institutionalizing Nature's Contributions to People: How a Chinese County Fabricated Tradeable Ecological Assets Through Sequential Governance Innovation
articles.abstract: The gap between ecosystem services valuation and practical monetization remains a persistent governance challenge, particularly in underdeveloped regions where ecological endowments are richest and fiscal capacity is weakest. This paper presents an in depth case study of Shangyou County, Jiangxi Province, China, which sequentially constructed an ecosystem services monetization architecture through four interlocking institutional mechanisms: (1) GEP/VEP accounting that rendered ecological value legible to financial actors; (2) a centralized state owned platform that consolidated scattered ecological resources; (3) market creating instruments, including China's first collapsed hill arable land indicator trade (RMB 344,960), the nation's second soil and water conservation carbon sink trade (70,000 tons CO₂e, RMB 2.1 million), and Jiangxi Province's largest VEP backed green loan (RMB 200 million); and (4) a zero carbon industrial park that anchored long term sustainability. Drawing on the IPBES framework of Nature's Contributions to People (NCP), the analysis reveals a cumulative sequential logic in which each mechanism created the preconditions for the next: measurement enabled consolidation, consolidation enabled market creation, and market creation provided the financial and policy foundation for industrial anchoring. The case contributes to the ecosystem services literature by demonstrating that the institutionalization of NCP is not a menu of independent options but a path dependent process in which sequencing determines outcomes. The findings carry implications for Payments for Ecosystem Services design, natural capital accounting, and the governance of green finance in developing economies.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Reinventing Well-being Measurements for Older Persons: Psychometric Evaluation of the Older Persons’ Well-being Scale (OPWELLS)
articles.authors: Jeanette Melin, Marit Preuter, Kristofer Årestedt, Marie-Louise Möllerberg
articles.abstract: Purpose: To establish a set of items with good content coverage and satisfactory measurement properties for measuring older persons’ well-being; the Older Persons’ Well-being Scale (OPWELLS). Method: A two-stage cross-sectional psychometric design was employed among older persons living in the community. In the first stage (n =441), the focus was on identifying potentially redundant items. In the second stage (n =1,882), stepwise item removal and/or alternation were conducted to optimise the balance between content coverage and measurement properties. Rasch analyses were used to evaluate measurement properties. Results: The first-stage study revealed issues primarily related to local dependency, leading to the removal of four items prior to the second-stage study. The second-stage study yielded a set of 10 items with good content representation and satisfactory measurement properties, as well as convergent validity with general health and life satisfaction. Conclusion: The 10-item OPWELLS provides a practical and psychometrically robust scale for measuring older persons’ well-being. Although there is no simple solution to challenges of limited content heterogeneity and insufficient measurement quality in existing well-being scales, this study represents an important step toward addressing both. By combining systematic content refinement with Rasch analysis, the OPWELLS contribute with a scale that strengthens the foundation for future research and practice.

articles.title: Executivism, the Parallel State, and the Paradoxical Path to Restoring Democratic Accountability
articles.abstract: Rebuilding democratic accountability after a period of personalist consolidation requires more than reversing executive orders or reinstating dismissed personnel. This paper argues that two interlocking structural conditions, executivism and the parallel state, made personalist consolidation possible under Trumpism and will outlast any single administration. Executivism is a bipartisan institutional zeitgeist that treats the presidency as the natural locus of policy production; the parallel state is the privatized governance infrastructure through which concentrated presidential authority is exercised. Together, they reinforce one another and are bound by a campaign-finance environment that links contracting to political contribution. I sketch a reform agenda organized around three institutional sites (the civil service and contracting infrastructure, Congress, and the judiciary) and confront the paradox that these reforms must be championed by a president willing to constrain the office.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: The SIMC Framework: Aligning Academic Research and Government Practice in Field Experiments
articles.abstract: Field experiments offer governments a credible route to causal evidence about what works. Yet translating experimental designs into practice remains difficult: researchers prioritize scientific rigor, while practitioners emphasize operational feasibility. We conceptualize this tension as an alignment problem. We introduce the SIMC framework (Scoping, Implementation, Measurement, and Communication), which identifies four stages where collaboration between researchers and practitioners can break down or succeed. Drawing on the COM-B model, we analyze the capability, opportunity, and motivation required to sustain collaboration. Using examples from field experiments in the U.S. and Europe, we show how trade-offs between rigor and feasibility emerge at each stage. We translate the framework into a stage-by-stage diagnostic that researchers and government agencies can apply before, during, and after fieldwork. Unlike existing guidance that treats collaboration as knowledge transfer, the SIMC framework treats it as an ongoing relational process with identifiable failure points.

articles.title: TRAJETÓRIAS ESCOLARES NO BRASIL ENTRE 2008 E 2018: UMA ANÁLISE DE SEQUÊNCIA
articles.authors: Estevão de Almeida Vilela, Bernardo L Queiroz
articles.abstract: Este artigo analisa as trajetórias escolares da coorte de estudantes ingressantes no 2º ano do ensino fundamental no Brasil em 2008, a partir de uma perspectiva de Curso de Vida Educacional, utilizando do arcabouço de análise de sequências. A classificação das trajetórias escolares tem sido realizada de forma arbitrária e menos eficaz em criar tipificações que caracterizam a complexidade do evento. Os resultados do artigo identificam padrões de trajetórias que revelam estudantes com percursos escolares estáveis e outros marcados por repetências e evasão em diferentes etapas da educação básica. Os resultados encontrados evidenciam a complexidade das trajetórias no Brasil e os padrões contribuem para um diagnóstico mais preciso da realidade educacional.

articles.title: A Measurement Framework for Detecting High-Velocity Events in Social Media
articles.abstract: This paper presents a measurement framework for detecting high-velocity events in social media using time-resolved post tracking. Rather than relying on static snapshots, the framework repeatedly samples posts over time and reconstructs their growth trajectories from timestamped observations. From these trajectories, rate-based metrics are derived to capture changes in engagement, including score and comment velocity. High-velocity events are defined as posts exhibiting extreme short-term growth rates relative to a fixed threshold, enabling systematic identification of burst-like behavior. Application of the framework to Reddit data shows that a small subset of posts exhibits rapid, high-intensity growth, while the majority evolve more gradually. These findings demonstrate that high-velocity events can be detected using simple, interpretable metrics, providing a foundation for studying temporal concentration and extreme dynamics in online systems.

articles.title: Inflectional Paradigms as a Diagnostic Tool for Tokenizers in Morphologically Rich Languages: A Proposal for a Linguistic Benchmark
articles.abstract: This article addresses the problem of evaluating the morphological quality of tokenizers used in natural language processing models for synthetic languages. The starting point is the observation that the standard measure of fertility ratio — the mean number of tokens per inflected form — captures the problem of fragmentation quantitatively but does not diagnose its morphological structure. A tokenizer achieving a low fertility ratio may nonetheless systematically cut morpheme boundaries at grammatically significant points, destroying information about the lexical stem, inflectional suffixes, or derivational prefixes. Based on empirical analysis of four tokenizers with different architectures (BPE, WordPiece, Unigram LM) and training corpus compositions (monolingual Polish, monolingual English, multilingual), conducted on 109 Polish inflected forms across full paradigms of 10 lexemes, this article proposes a linguistic benchmark based on three metrics: stem integrity (SI), inflectional suffix separation (ISS), and mean fragmentation per lexeme (MFL). Results show a 3.2x ratio between the monolingual Polish tokenizer (HerBERT BPE-PL, MFL = 1.22) and the monolingual English tokenizer (GPT-2 BPE-EN, MFL = 3.91), and indicate that tokenizer architecture has independent explanatory power: XLM-R Unigram (MFL = 1.66) with a multilingual training corpus approaches the monolingual tokenizer more closely than mBERT WordPiece (MFL = 2.45) with comparable data composition. The proposed benchmark is designed as minimal yet sufficient: aspect and conditional mood do not require separate diagnostic categories, because their behavior follows structurally from the behavior of the prefix and suffix that the benchmark measures directly. Inferring these categories from a correctly tokenized form is a task for the language model, not the tokenizer. This article continues research published in the preprint Dawidek (2026a) and constitutes a proof of concept for an evaluation tool that fills the gap between performance-based and linguistic assessment of tokenizers in morphologically rich languages.

articles.title: Perceptions of the Ideal Mother: An Experimental Investigation
articles.authors: Nevena Kulic, Maria-Valentina Stoicescu, Anne-Marie Therese Jeannet, Giulia Dotti Sani
articles.abstract: The normative framework that defines ideal motherhood, prescribing what it means to be a “good mother” in a given cultural and historical context, significantly influences women’s career aspirations, family choices, and fertility decisions. Yet, there is limited understanding of what women themselves consider to be “ideal motherhood”, particularly given that such ideals may reflect both genuine preferences and adaptations to structural constraints. Gaining insight into both personal and perceived societal ideals is crucial for evaluating the extent to which women’s views of motherhood align with, or diverge from, prevailing social expectations. This study builds on hegemonic and non-hegemonic models of motherhood differing in the degree of maternal involvement, the role of maternal employment, and the division of childcare responsibilities. We conduct a large-scale conjoint experiment embedded in an online survey administered in autumn 2024, involving approximately 4,500 Italian women aged 20 to 50, to evaluate the relative importance of various attributes associated with different models of motherhood. The findings show that women tend to favor elements of counter-hegemonic motherhood models over hegemonic ones, both in their personal ideals and in their perceptions of societal expectations. In particular, maternal employment (whether full- or part-time) and the sharing of childcare responsibilities with fathers emerge as central components of the ideal mother. These results challenge conventional assumptions about women’s endorsement of the dominant notion of mothering, offering new insight into shifting motherhood ideals in contemporary Italy.

articles.title: The Preprint Problem Problem: Response to Giangrande
articles.abstract: Giangrande (2026) argues that fringe scientists have “weaponized” preprints to spread pseudoscience, and presents data to prove the point. In a response, I identify two major problems in the paper. The first is that the data do not match their description, and the second is that description of how preprint servers work – and thus the mechanism of action for the problem – is inaccurate. In addition, the documentation provided with the paper is inadequate for assessment. The paper does not establish the prevalence of weaponized pseudoscience on preprint servers beyond an existence proof. I conclude that the paper cannot live up to its inflammatory title, “The Preprint Problem” – but scientists do have an important role to play in protecting our communities from the bad actors of pseudoscience.

articles.title: Intergroup Violence Without Essentialist Beliefs: The Case of Turkana Cattle Raiders
articles.abstract: Essentialism has been hypothesized to be associated with intergroup hostility. However, it is unclear whether ethnic essentialism is a feature of human social cognition across a range of socio-ecological settings and what the causal direction of its association with intergroup hostility is. One possibility is that essentialist beliefs cause intergroup violence because they make violence on members of out-groups more palatable. An alternative hypothesis is that essentialist beliefs may develop as a response to intergroup violence. Using “switched at birth” vignettes to assess how intergenerationally inherited and fixed people’s identities are, we assessed the extent to which Turkana pastoralist warriors have essentialist beliefs about themselves and the neighboring Toposa ethnic group in South Sudan. The Turkana and Toposa engage in mutually lethal cattle raids, which account for about half of adult Turkana male mortality in our study area. We found that only four of 162 (2.5%) Turkana surveyed expressed essentialist beliefs about themselves and the Toposa, suggesting that ethnic essentialism may not be a cross-culturally widespread feature of social cognition nor a necessary ideology for engaging in intergroup hostilities. The four participants who expressed essentialist beliefs did not exhibit unusual combat-related behavior and attitudes towards the outgroup. Specifically, they were no different than non-essentialist survey respondents in their levels of combat exposure, killing of enemies in combat, witnessing morally injurious events, and moral judgments about killing Toposa. However, one of the four was an outlier in offensive raiding and killing, anecdotally suggesting that essentialism may contribute to, but be insufficient for, very prolific raiding in individual Turkana. The cultural similarity between the Toposa and Turkana, a common origin story, well-known examples of cross-group adoption and assimilation, and a tradition of cross-border friendships may explain the Turkana’s low frequency of essentialist beliefs despite widespread lethal violence.

articles.title: Peace, War, and the Spirit of Commerce: Genovesi as a reader of Montesquieu
articles.abstract: What are the consequences of trade among nations? In De l’esprit des lois, Montesquieu claimed that the spirit of commerce fosters peace, restraining conflict by leading nations to collaborate in pursuit of mutual benefit. Economic interests, he suggested, channel and moderate the violent impulses of human passions. In contrast, the Neapolitan philosopher and economist Antonio Genovesi offered a strikingly different interpretation. In his reading of Montesquieu, Genovesi argued that the spirit of commerce was itself a spirit of conquest—an engine of rivalry and domination among nations. Yet, he maintained, the telos of trade rests upon an alternative spirit, one of mutual aid, capable of nurturing peace and cooperation both within and between societies. This paper offers a philosophical analysis of the dialogue between Montesquieu and Genovesi, tracing how their exchange shaped Genovesi’s evolving reflections on the intricate relationship between commerce and war.

articles.title: Negotiating the Good Mother Ideal: Maternal Guilt in Contemporary Society
articles.authors: Maria-Valentina Stoicescu, Natalia Soboleva, Nevena Kulic, Giulia Dotti Sani
articles.abstract: This narrative review integrates quantitative and qualitative research to provide a comprehensive overview of the phenomenon of maternal guilt, examining its measurement, types, triggers, and prevalence. Maternal guilt is a widespread emotion that is associated with the societal role of women as caregivers, yet this association is sometimes lost when talking more broadly about parental guilt. Critical gaps persist in the understanding and measurement of maternal guilt, particularly in the areas of cross-cultural variation, intersectional perspectives, and the long-term consequences for both mothers and their children. The classification of maternal guilt into types highlights that maternal guilt can take different forms, depending on specific root causes and circumstances. By analysing triggers, this review also shows that maternal guilt is enhanced through individual, situational, and institutional factors, and it varies across countries, being influenced by societal and cultural characteristics.

articles.title: Using Statistical Modelling and Geographic Data to Challenge Declinist Headlines - a Case Study of Fish and Chip Shops in Great Britain
articles.abstract: The traditional fish and chip shop is an important part of the food environment in many UK neighbourhoods. However, rising costs, changing tastes and competition have resulted in fish and chip shop closures, with some alarmist predictions that up to half of all shops could close in a single year. Using Points of Interest data and multilevel modelling, we look beyond the headlines and model trends in fish and chip shop provision by neighbourhood type between 2022 and 2025. We identify an overall decline in the number of shops yet find no evidence to support the predictions of widespread closures. However, closures have been unevenly spread across neighbourhood types, with the greatest decline in deprived and ethnically diverse urban areas, and less impact on some more affluent neighbourhoods with older populations. Although some adaption may be needed to maintain viable amidst rising costs, our findings suggest that the traditional fish and chip shop remains an important part of the food environment in many neighbourhoods. In conclusion, we highlight the importance of robust data driven insight when considering the changing provision of services and comment on how these approaches could be applied in other contexts.

articles.title: Teaching Teachers
articles.authors: Danilo Kuzmanic, Per Engzell, John Jerrim
articles.abstract: Good teachers are role models. Using Chilean administrative data, we estimate how teacher value-added (VA) affects the VA of students who later become teachers. Exposure to a math teacher with 1 SD higher VA raises teacher VA in the next generation by 0.14 SD and student achievement one generation removed by 0.04 SD. Effects compound with longer exposure and appear driven by observable classroom practices. Under plausible assumptions, these spillovers raise cumulative returns to instructional quality by 28 percent after one generation and 39 percent in the long run.

articles.title: Industry Differences in Upward Mobility from Low-Wage Labour Market Entry
articles.abstract: A low-wage occupation is a common entry point into the labour market, yet the trajectories that follow vary considerably. This study examines whether patterns of upward mobility from low-wage labour market entry differ across industries. Using Swedish full population register data, the analysis follows all individuals aged 18–25 who entered the labour market in 2003 in low wage occupations and traces their occupational trajectories over 18 years. Event history models are used to estimate the timing of transitions from low wage to higher wage occupations. The results indicate substantial and persistent differences across industries in both the pace and likelihood of upward mobility. Workers in manufacturing and construction tend to move into higher wage occupations more quickly and more often, whereas those in hospitality, cleaning, and personal services experience markedly slower transitions. In several of these service industries, workers remain in low wage occupations more than twice as long as comparable workers in manufacturing, and a sizeable share do not transition at all within the observation period. As these service industries expand their share of low wage jobs—and as women and immigrants remain disproportionately concentrated within them—these patterns raise concerns about social stratification. The findings highlight how industrial structures shape early career mobility and interact with broader labour market inequalities, underscoring the importance of industrial opportunity structures for understanding trajectories from low-wage work.

articles.title: Between the Embedding and the Prompt: Systematic Design Effects in LLM-based Occupation Coding
articles.authors: Olga Kononykhina, Anna-Carolina Haensch, Frauke Kreuter
articles.abstract: Assigning free-text job descriptions to standardised taxonomies is a persistent bottleneck in survey research and official statistics. Large language models (LLMs) offer a promising path toward automation, but each step in the pipeline involves both model architecture and measurement choices about how an occupation should be represented. Through 119 experiments on German survey data, we systematically vary the textual representation of occupational categories, embedding models, LLMs, and prompt design. Category representation changes retrieval accuracy by 8–23 percentage points and classification by 11–21. Prompt role and abstention behaviour are model-specific and must be validated before deployment. The dominant source of variance, however, sits outside model measurement choices. How respondents describe their work matters more than any model or design choice (ICC = 0.76).

articles.title: TOWARD MORPHOLOGICALLY-AWARE TOKENIZATION FOR INFLECTIONAL LANGUAGES: A STRUCTURAL HYPOTHESIS
articles.abstract: This article proposes a theoretical hypothesis concerning the structural and temporal architecture of tokenization in inflectional languages. The starting point is an observation documented in prior empirical work (Dawidek, 2026a; 2026b): standard frequency-based tokenization systematically fails to preserve morphologically significant boundaries in Polish, a synthetic inflectional language. The present article argues that this failure has a deeper cause than training data composition or algorithm choice. Standard tokenization has no preconditions — it begins cutting without structural knowledge of the word — and no concept of time: it treats the interval between cuts as empty latency rather than as a potential carrier of morphological information. I propose two interconnected hypotheses. First, that linguistically motivated tokenization requires a morphological skeleton as a precondition — a minimal representation of root, prefix, ending type, case, number, gender, and valency, specified here for Polish and hypothesized to generalize to other inflectional languages. Second, that the cutting order itself is morphologically motivated and temporally structured: prefix first, inflectional ending second, root exposed last — where exposure is not a cut but a revelation of what was present throughout. I further propose the concept of morphological resolution time — the interval between cuts understood as a site of grammatical resolution rather than computational waste. This proposal has architectural consequences: the temporal dimension required by the hypothesis is structurally incompatible with standard transformer parallelism. A hybrid architecture combining sequential morphological pre-processing with parallel downstream processing is identified as a research direction. This article presents a hypothesis, not demonstrated results. It is offered as a theoretical framework for empirical work that has not yet been done — and as a named set of variables that must exist before measurement can begin.

articles.title: Social Entropy, Adaptive Coherence, and Transcendent Detachment: An Integrative Framework for Measuring Coherence and Fragility in Human Systems
articles.abstract: Human groups, institutions, relationships, and identities can be modeled as dynamic information-processing systems that require continual maintenance to pre serve coherence under uncertainty. This article develops Adaptive Coherence and Entropic Attachment (ACEA), an integrative framework linking information theory, cybernetics, resilience science, attachment theory, stress appraisal, predictive processing, allostasis, discourse analysis, and institutional theory. The framework formalizes social coherence as a time-dependent state variable shaped by trust, shared knowledge, relational cohesion, communication clarity, adaptive capacity, regenerative repair, informational noise, fear, uncertainty, external disturbance, and attachment rigidity. The central contribution is a testable account of “transcendent detachment” as adaptive non-rigidity: the capacity to preserve meaning, commitment, and identity without requiring absolute permanence or total control. ACEA is not presented as a finished empirical law, but as a formal research program that generates measurable constructs, falsifiable hypotheses, and practical applications for discourse analysis, organizational resilience, conflict mediation, institutional design, and psychological adaptation.

articles.title: The Origin of Patriarchal Moral System: An Evolutionary Model
articles.abstract: [This is a new submission replacing a previous version that was accidentally deleted. The earlier submission was declined due to an incomplete ORCID profile; the profile has now been updated with full professional information, qualifications, and biography. The manuscript is currently under peer review at the journal Human Nature and is not AI-generated.] This article proposes an evolutionary-materialist model for the origin of the patriarchal moral system — a set of normative prohibitions restricting divorce, female sexuality, and freedom of mate choice. The model takes as its starting point the reproductive strategy of male provisioning — the exchange of male‑provided resources for female reproductive access — shaped by sexual selection. It is shown that the transition from foraging to civilization generates three demographic problems that make this strategy unstable: (a) increased population density facilitating partner switching, (b) awareness of the causal link between intercourse and childbirth reducing female motivation for sex, and (c) dissociation between the timing of sexual and social maturity in males. The normative solution to each of these problems—and their combination as a patriarchal moral system—is proposed to be understood as an adaptation to new demographic conditions. The model explains the convergent emergence of similar prohibitions across different cultures and the specific role of older males in enforcing them. The article is theoretical in nature and can serve as a foundation for further empirical research.

articles.title: Naming under constraint: gender, care and symbolic power in family life
articles.abstract: This article proposes a reconceptualisation of children’s surname attribution as a gendered site of symbolic power within heterosexual family life. While naming is commonly framed as a private and consensual decision, feminist scholarship has shown that family practices are structured by persistent asymmetries of care, authority, and recognition. Yet the symbolic dimension of naming remains underexamined. Building on feminist theories of care, symbolic violence, and situated knowledge, this article introduces the concept of naming under constraint to analyse how naming decisions are produced under conditions that limit deliberation and agency. Drawing on a case in Portugal, it shows how a naming decision taken immediately after childbirth - under bodily vulnerability, institutional urgency, and unequal care relations - becomes stabilised as a consensual outcome. The article further develops the notion of delayed articulation to theorise the temporal gap between living inequality and naming it as such, highlighting how care-intensive life stages constrain feminist critique. By shifting attention from naming as a neutral outcome to naming as a situated process, this article contributes to sociological debates on gender, care, and symbolic power, and highlights the importance of attending to the temporal and relational conditions under which inequality becomes intelligible.

articles.title: The Silence of Moderates Drives Social Polarization: An Agent-Based Model of Perceived and Actual Polarization
articles.abstract: Social polarization increasingly undermines the collaborative dialogue essential to democratic societies, necessitating a clearer understanding of the mechanisms behind its emergence and resolution. Traditional research has explained polarization through interpersonal repulsion, in which individuals move their opinions away from different views (e.g., Axelrod et al., 2021). While this framework focuses on conflicts among active speakers, it overlooks the silence and withdrawal of moderates. We developed a model that excludes repulsion and incorporates the silence gap effect, in which individuals remain silent toward those with stronger attitudes. Simulations show that in the early stages, the silence gap effect causes moderates to remain silent while allowing extreme opinions to dominate public expressions. This generates perceived polarization, a state in which the distribution of public opinion becomes bimodal despite unimodal internal attitudes. Subsequently, the frequently expressed extreme opinions pull other agents toward the poles, eventually leading to polarization. These findings demonstrate that the essence of polarization lies in the silence of moderates. Furthermore, our study shows that polarization can be prevented by neutralizing the silence gap effect, ensuring that moderates do not suppress opinions. However, we found that even slight differences in intervention timing determined whether future polarization was successfully averted. By contrast, interventions aimed at correcting the misperception of a polarized public failed to prevent division. Therefore, to resolve polarization, it is vital to create a dialogue environment in which moderates can speak as early as possible without apprehension, rather than directly addressing extreme voices or promoting the correction of public perception.

articles.title: Image Abstraction and Bayesian Inference: A Reconstruction of the Nature of Probability Theory from the Perspective of Cognitive Philosophy
articles.abstract: This paper demonstrates, from an epistemological standpoint, the structural isomorphism between Quxiang Bilei—the classical Chinese cognitive method of image abstraction and analogical reasoning, literally "taking phenomena to form analogies and categorical associations"—and Bayesian inference. By systematically mapping the three components of Bayes's theorem—prior probability, likelihood function, and posterior probability—onto the three constitutive stages of Quxiang Bilei: image-taking, analogy-making, and verification, this paper reveals that Bayesian inference is not merely a specialized technical toolset, but rather the mathematical formalization of the metacognitive structure inherent in Quxiang Bilei. Building upon this isomorphic framework, the paper advances five core philosophical propositions: (1) objectivity is the statistical convergence outcome of collective Bayesian updating across epistemic communities; (2) causal laws represent the limiting case in which posterior probability infinitely approaches unity; (3) the interpretability of probabilistic calculations resides not within mathematics itself, but in its correspondence with underlying metacognitive structures; (4) the absolute boundary of artificial intelligence lies not in insufficient computational power, but in its lack of embodied physical anchoring and authentic survival feedback; and (5) there exists a fundamental, non-accidental isomorphism between Quxiang Bilei and Bayesian inference. These five propositions are not mere mutual projections between two disparate conceptual frameworks, but genuinely novel insights emerging from their productive dialogue and critical engagement. This work provides a robust epistemological foundation for Bayesianism, offers rigorous mathematical support for the modern transformation of classical Chinese epistemology, and establishes a unified analytical framework for understanding the cognitive essence and fundamental limitations of artificial intelligence.

articles.title: Finite-Capacity Prospect Theory: State-Dependent Risk Preferences under Resource, Attention, and Boundary-Risk Constraints
articles.abstract: This paper develops a state-dependent version of Prospect Theory in which loss aversion, reference adaptation, and probability weighting vary with resource buffers, attention constraints, and boundary-risk context. Prospect Theory is usually treated as a descriptive account of deviations from expected-utility rationality. This paper proposes a finite-capacity bridge model: bounded agents operating under scarcity, limited attention, finite memory, and finite update capacity should exhibit state-dependent risk preferences. The model introduces a vector-valued resource buffer, perceived distance to a constraint threshold, event-class-dependent probability weighting, and a nudge-bandwidth model. It predicts that loss aversion should rise under scarcity and downside exposure, reference baselines should adapt more slowly under depletion, and probability weighting should become more distorted for low-capacity agents and boundary-relevant rare events. The paper includes deterministic synthetic simulations and a replication package, but does not fit human-subject data. It is intended as a theoretical working paper and empirical identification framework for future tests of state-dependent Prospect Theory.

articles.title: Strategic Heterogeneity in Issue Positioning: Evidence from Congressional Campaigns
articles.abstract: Do candidates maintain consistent left-right issue positions in congressional elections? How do district conditions influence variations in this consistency? While these questions are fundamental to the study of U.S. elections, surprisingly little is known about issue positioning in campaigns. We combine campaign platforms with machine learning methods to estimate the left-right orientations of U.S. House candidates (n = 4,509) across six salient issue areas in multiple election cycles (2018-2022). Our validated measures reveal meaningful within-candidate variation in issue positioning that systematically reflects district conditions. Candidates exhibit greater positional heterogeneity in districts that are not safe for their party and where constituent policy preferences are variable. We further demonstrate that this positional variation directly reflects district opinion, as issue-specific positioning aligns with constituency policy preferences. These findings reveal how candidate messaging varies systematically across issue domains and electoral contexts, fundamentally shaping the information environment that structures democratic choice and representation.

articles.title: Securitized Symbolic Boundaries: Muslim Identity, Terrorism Discourse, and Public Opinion in Switzerland
articles.authors: Anina Schwarzenbach, Livia Zöbeli, Emirhan Darcan
articles.abstract: How do democratic societies embed religious difference within the logic of security governance? This article introduces the concept of securitized symbolic boundaries to analyze how religious and cultural distinctions become organized and sustained as matters of existential threat within public discourse. Building on symbolic boundary theory and securitization scholarship, we examine the discursive construction of Islam and Muslim identity within Swiss media coverage of extremism and terrorism between 1994 and 2024. Drawing on a corpus of 286,660 articles from 26 outlets, we combine topic modeling, Bayesian change-point detection, and interrupted time-series analysis to trace the structure, dynamics, and consequences of securitized boundary-making over three decades. We show that Muslim identity is disproportionately and distinctively linked to security frames relative to other religious identities within a corpus designed to capture extremist discourse across religious variants equally. This disproportionality is organized through three recurrent frames: religious governance, visible religious practices, and migration-related otherness. Crisis events—including major European terrorist attacks and domestic policy initiatives—function as critical junctures that reorganize rather than merely intensify this discourse, with the relative salience of frames shifting systematically across discursive phases. During such periods, ideologically diverse outlets temporarily converge on shared threat narratives, producing what we term crisis consensus: a narrowing of interpretive diversity that intensifies symbolic exclusion. Although these surges are episodic, elements of crisis framing persist, reshaping baseline discourse over time in ways consistent with long-term embedding rather than post-crisis equilibrium restoration. Linking these media patterns to repeated cross-sectional survey data, we find that periods of intensified securitized coverage align with more exclusionary public attitudes toward Muslims, with associations driven by overall coverage prominence rather than the content of specific frames. Together, the findings show how security discourse can transform religious difference into a durable symbolic boundary. The article identifies three mechanisms behind this process: boundary brightening during crises, temporary convergence across ideologically diverse outlets, and the persistence of crisis frames after acute events subside.

articles.title: Quantum Coherence in Neural Microtubules: A Unified, Empirically Grounded, and Testable Framework for Gamma Oscillation Precision
articles.abstract: Gamma-band oscillations (30-100 Hz) exhibit timing precision that challenges strictly classical accounts of neural synchronization. This manuscript presents a unified the- oretical framework proposing that quantum coherence in neural microtubules may serve as a modulatory mechanism to enhance gamma oscillation precision. We provide: (1) rigorous, step-by-step decoherence derivations constrained by thermal, electromagnetic, and mechanical channels; (2) empirically grounded parameters derived from microtubule electromagnetic oscillations and tryptophan superradiance experiments; (3) a conservative quantum-classical coupling mechanism that modulates pyramidal-interneuron network gamma (PING) and interneuron network gamma (ING) precision through weak electromagnetic fields (∼nT); (4) complete experimental designs integrating nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center quantum sensing with high-density electrophysiology; and (5) computational validation pipelines us- ing finite element modeling and stochastic simulations. We formalize the Perry Constant (κ ≈ 1.7±0.3 ms−1) as a proposed quantitative bridge linking coherence factor to precision enhancement. Our framework generates four primary testable predictions: measurable coherence-precision correlations (r >0.3), quantum-consistent temperature scaling (Tc ≈ 12 ± 3 K), resonance-selective electromagnetic eects (Q > 5 in 4060 Hz), and pharmacological selectivity for microtubule-targeting drugs. We transparently identify two major quantitative gaps in the initial framework: a ∼6 order-of-magnitude discrepancy between calculated eective coherence times (∼15 ns) and required functional timescales (∼ms), and an electric coupling energy (ΔV ≈ 3.2 μV) below ion channel threshold (∼4 mV). We then resolve these gaps through four physically justied mechanisms supported by novel simulations: (1) non-Markovian dynamics with structured spectral density extend coherence 10- 33× (to ∼165500 ns); (2) cooperative gating across ∼100 channels plus stochastic resonance amplication achieves ∼40% gating modulation; (3) lattice geometry simulation conrms collective decoherence scaling; and (4) phase-coherent accumulation over gamma cycles enables network-level detection (SNR ≈ 143). A revised PING network incorporating all mechanisms produces clear 40 Hz gamma locking (spike PLV = 0.74). Alternative classical explanations are systematically discussed and distinguished through quantum-specic signatures. This work represents a falsifiable, empirically testable contribution to quantum biology and neuroscience, avoiding speculative claims about consciousness generation while advancing our understanding of neural timing precision. Keywords: Quantum Coherence, Microtubules, Gamma Oscillations, Decoherence, Neural Precision, NV-Center Sensing, Quantum Biology, Perry Constant, Mathematical Modeling, Neuroscience

subjects.name: Oceanography and Atmospheric Sciences and Meteorology

articles.title: How do opinion-based and social group identities influence each other? Evidence from a survey experiment on class and transnational political issues
articles.abstract: Do people identify with social groups because of their political opinions, or do they identify with political opinion camps because of their basis in social groups? We build a theoretical model of why opinion-based and social group-based polarization are connected and test this with a survey experiment embedded in the British Election Study Internet Panel. The opinion-based identities concern immigration and the environment, both strongly connected to the universalist-particularist cleavage. We social group identity focused on is the working class, which has considerable salience in the British context. When self-identifying working class respondents are informed that their class is the majority in an opinion-based group, they feel closer to that group. However, specific opinion-camps feel more distant from the working class when they believe this class is the majority in the opposing opinion-based group. Social group and opinion-based polarization are thus intertwined, one influencing the other.

articles.title: Low health-information discernment as a public-health vulnerability in Europe: evidence from an eight-country survey
articles.authors: Laurent Cordonier, Gérald Bronner, Florian Cafiero
articles.abstract: Background: During health emergencies, people face an overabundance of information of varying reliability. Their responses to health-authority recommendations may depend on their general ability to discern accurate from inaccurate health claims. In this study, we examined the distribution and correlates of low health-information discernment in eight European countries and its links with acceptance of recommended health measures in a hypothetical pandemic scenario. Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional online survey of 24,203 adults in France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, Italy, Poland, Spain, and Sweden. Respondents read a hypothetical pandemic scenario and reported their acceptance within that scenario of three recommended health measures: following preventive behaviours, taking a new antiviral treatment if infected, and receiving a new vaccine. Health-information discernment was measured as the difference between mean endorsement of accurate and inaccurate health claims unrelated to the pandemic scenario. Low discernment was defined as being in the lowest quartile of the pooled discernment distribution. We used country-specific raking weights and fitted adjusted regression models to estimate low-discernment prevalence, identify its correlates, and assess its associations with acceptance of the three recommended measures. We also estimated adjusted risk differences in low or uncertain acceptance for respondents with low health-information discernment versus all other respondents, and conducted country-stratified and sensitivity analyses. Findings: Low health-information discernment, defined as the lowest quartile of the pooled distribution, varied substantially across countries, from 18.2% in Sweden to 36.0% in Hungary. In adjusted analyses, low discernment was more likely among respondents with higher alternative therapy orientation (OR 1.76, 95% CI 1.66–1.86), higher trust in weak or non-institutional health sources (OR 1.70, 95% CI 1.60–1.81), and higher New Age orientation (OR 1.24, 95% CI 1.18–1.30). By contrast, it was less likely among respondents with higher trust in institutional health sources (OR 0.51, 95% CI 0.49–0.54) and higher trust in science and medicine (OR 0.69, 95% CI 0.66–0.73). Low health-information discernment was associated with lower acceptance of all three recommended measures in adjusted models using continuous acceptance scores: preventive behaviours (-0.32, 95% CI -0.35 to -0.30), antiviral treatment (-0.13, 95% CI -0.15 to -0.10), and vaccination (-0.13, 95% CI -0.16 to -0.10). In separate adjusted models using binary outcomes, low discernment was associated with a higher adjusted risk of low or uncertain acceptance of each measure. This risk was higher by 12.6 percentage points for preventive behaviours, 5.7 percentage points for antiviral treatment, and 5.1 percentage points for vaccination.

articles.title: Between Perceived Fairness and Resignation: Examining Machine Heuristics, Privacy Cynicism and Self-Disclosure in Generative AI
articles.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) systems such as ChatGPT and DeepSeek require users to disclose nuanced prompts that may reveal highly sensitive personal information. Existing studies on privacy risks in generative AI are fragmented and rarely grounded in established privacy theories or comparative frameworks. To advance knowledge on generative AI privacy, a multi‑stage model of privacy decision‑making is proposed that integrates machine heuristics, privacy concerns, privacy cynicism and self‑disclosure. Drawing on two online surveys in China and the US (N = 1632), we examine how machine heuristic perceptions relate to privacy concerns, how concerns translate into privacy cynicism, and how cynicism is associated with self‑disclosure. Chinese respondents reported higher self‑disclosure, whereas US participants expressed greater privacy concerns, mistrust and uncertainty, but also higher self‑efficacy and use frequency. The findings suggest that perceptions of AI as fair, accurate, and having expertise are associated with lower privacy concerns, that concerns are positively linked to cynicism dimensions, and that cynicism exhibits divergent associations with self‑disclosure. These results are discussed and contextualized in relation to privacy scholarship and policy and design recommendation.

articles.title: Alarms Without Smoke: Predicting and Addressing False Alarms in Urban Fire Services
articles.abstract: False fire alarms waste essential resources and prevent fire services from responding to real fires in time. While previous research has mainly focused on improving hardware accuracy, the spatial and temporal patterns of false alarms remain largely unexplored. This study analyses 15 years of historical fire incident data from the West Midlands Fire Service, UK. The data reveal that attending false alarms accounts for 34% of total mobilisation time. Given this significant potential for resource savings, we applied machine learning to predict an alarm’s validity at the point of dispatch. Our proposed model achieves an accuracy of 86.0% and only misses 2.1% of real incidents. This study introduces a new geospatial perspective to mitigate the impact of false alarms. Furthermore, the model can be integrated into existing fire service policies to enhance public safety and urban resilience.

articles.title: Foreign Exchange Intervention: Identification and Evidence
articles.abstract: Foreign exchange intervention is widely used across low, middle and high income economies, yet its effectiveness remains disputed. The central difficulty is identification: interventions are typically endogenous to exchange rate movements, often bundled with other policies and sometimes unobserved. This survey reviews five decades of empirical research and reframes the debate as a problem of causal identification rather than a binary question of whether intervention works. The literature is organized by identification strategy: event studies, reaction-function residuals, controlled regressions, instrumental variables, high-frequency approaches, SVARs, structural portfolio-balance models and natural experiments. For each design, the source of identifying variation and the key assumptions required for causal interpretation are specified. Differences in reported effectiveness largely reflect differences in design, data and regime context. The overall finding is that intervention does affect exchange rates: evidence is strongest for short-run effects on returns and volatility, especially when operations are large, well-timed and implemented in thinner markets. Persistent exchange rate level effects are more sensitive to assumptions about asset substitutability, expectations and financial frictions. The survey provides guidance for empirical practice and highlights priorities for improved measurement and future research.

articles.title: Assessing Foreign Interference During the "Year of Elections"
articles.authors: Kevin T. Greene, Jeffrey J. Imbrogno, Jacob N Shapiro
articles.abstract: How widespread was foreign interference in elections in 2024, the "global year of elections"? We compile new data on foreign election interference in each of the 63 national-level executive and legislative elections held in 2024. We find that foreign interference was common, there is clear evidence of it in 30 percent of elections and affecting 60 percent of the world’s voting population. Russia conducted the most interference efforts, targeting more elections than all other actors combined, and focusing largely on European elections. Interference was most often carried out through social media and by setting up fake news sites.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Data Resource Profile: Nursing and Midwifery Council Register Linked to Census 2021- England and Wales
articles.authors: Michelle Jamieson, Nathan O'Connor, Caroline Kenny, Iain Maitland Atherton
articles.abstract: Introduction: Nurses, midwives, and nursing associates form the largest group of registered health professionals in the United Kingdom. Research into the social circumstances of this workforce has been constrained by the absence of population-level data linking professional registration to sociodemographic characteristics. The NMC Register–Census 2021 linked dataset for England and Wales addresses this gap. Methods: The Nursing and Midwifery Council (NMC) provided five data extracts: cross-sectional extracts of the professional register as of March 2018, 2021, 2022, and 2023, and a leavers extract covering 2018–2021. A pseudonymised personal registration number links individuals across all five extracts. The Office for National Statistics (ONS) linked each extract to the 2021 England and Wales Census using deterministic and probabilistic record linkage methods. Results: The rebuilt 2021 register comprises 732,835 distinct registrants across all four UK nations. Of 614,975 registrants resident in England or Wales at census night and eligible for linkage, 565,680 (91.9%) were successfully linked to the Census. The linked dataset encompasses individual-level registration, sociodemographic, socioeconomic, household, migration, and geographic variables. Conclusion: The NMC-Census 2021 linked dataset for England and Wales is an ADR UK flagship resource enabling population-level analysis of the nursing and midwifery workforce and its social determinants. It supports research into workforce composition, retention, commuting geography, and longitudinal change. Annual additions of the NMC register data are planned. Accredited researchers may apply for access via the ONS Secure Research Service.

articles.title: Belief and Resistance: Vulnerability to Authoritarian Disinformation in Turkey and the Effect of Counter-Strategies
articles.authors: Aykut Öztürk, Seda Demiralp, Ugur Ozdemir, Candas Ayan
articles.abstract: Contemporary authoritarian governments increasingly rely on disinformation as a tool of political control. While a growing literature examines the supply side of authoritarian disinformation, far less is known about how citizens engage with it at the individual level. We address this question, taking Turkey as a critical case. Drawing on an original survey fielded during the first two weeks of the disinformation campaign that followed the arrest of Istanbul Mayor Ekrem İmamoğlu in March 2025, we move beyond the conventional focus on partisan attitudes to offer a more comprehensive account of individual-level vulnerability. We show that epistemic character traits, stable dispositions that structure how individuals relate to truth and knowledge, are consistent predictors of disinformation susceptibility, with effects that hold across partisan lines. A pre-registered survey experiment embedded in the same survey reveals that fact-checking modestly reduces belief in disinformation narratives, while a media literacy prime backfires, increasing endorsement of regime narratives. These findings advance our understanding of how authoritarian disinformation operates, who is most vulnerable to it, and what kinds of interventions are likely to help or harm, with implications for both the study of authoritarian politics and the design of counter-disinformation policy.

articles.title: Manufacturing for Economic Growth, Industrial Relations 2026, and Comprehensive National Power: A Critical Assessment
articles.abstract: This paper examines the Industrial Relations (Central) Rules, 2026, notified by the Ministry of Labour and Employment on 8 May 2026 through Gazette notification G.S.R. 342(E), and assesses their implications for manufacturing-led growth and Comprehensive National Power. The analysis situates the rules within the scholarly debate between Bhagwati and Panagariya on labour-intensive manufacturing, Rajan on domestic-oriented growth, and Rodrik on premature deindustrialization. Four provisions are documented: the 300-worker threshold for prior permission on lay-offs, retrenchments, and closures; the 14-day strike notice with a 60-day validity period applicable across all establishments; the 30 percent secret-ballot threshold for sole negotiating union recognition; and the Worker Re-skilling Fund under Rule 37. A comparative review of labour regimes in China, Vietnam, Bangladesh, Indonesia, the United States, the United Kingdom, and Germany indicates that India retains a prior-permission architecture for collective dismissals while peer jurisdictions operate on notice-and-consult models. The paper applies Bhonsle's framework on Comprehensive National Power to read the rules as a mechanism mediating economic, governance, social-harmony, and human-capital objectives, and identifies limits in coverage, digital access, and unemployment protection.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Global Credit Cycles and Pro-Business Rhetoric in Party Manifestos
articles.authors: Timm Betz, Andrew Kerner, Cristina Bodea
articles.abstract: We identify a novel pathway that links financial globalization to politics. We emphasize the effect of globalization on the relationships between governments and domestic business owners, who, like all borrowers, are subject to “Global Financial Cycles” originating from U.S. credit conditions. Downturns in these cycles, stemming from high U.S. interest rates, reduce credit availability, depress asset prices, and broadly worsen the outlook for private sector profits.
