While politicians have limited power to address the underlying financial conditions, they can adopt business-friendly politics to signal their willingness to compensate firms for the higher borrowing costs created from higher U.S. interest rates. We support our argument with evidence from party manifestos across 59 countries, covering 1963 to 2017. Our paper documents a new connection between global capital cycles and party positions during an era of largely unrestricted capital mobility.

articles.title: УПРАВЛЕНИЕ КРЕДИТНЫМИ РИСКАМИ В БРОКЕРСКИХ КОМПАНИЯХ
articles.abstract: Данная статья исследует современные подходы к эффективному управлению кредитными рисками в контексте работы брокерских компаний. Автор представляет систематический анализ методов и инструментов, необходимых для оценки и управления кредитными рисками, связанными с брокерской деятельностью. Особое внимание уделяется разработке моделей и стратегий минимизации рисков, а также внедрению современных технологий и аналитических методов. Статья содержит ценные практические рекомендации для брокерских компаний, стремящихся укрепить свою финансовую устойчивость и обеспечить безопасность клиентских средств.

articles.title: Co-creating community-driven LGBTQ+ health and research priorities in Mongolia
articles.authors: Dorjjantsan Ganbaatar, Thiago Melo Santos, Nicholas Hill, Cathy Vaughan, Enkhmaa Enkhbold, Erdeneburen Dorjpurev, Sugarmaa Myagmarjav, Meghan Bohren
articles.abstract: LGBTQ+ research and policy in Mongolia largely focused on sexually transmitted infections (STIs), particularly among men who have sex with men (MSM). This narrow focus has resulted in a significant lack of evidence on the specific health needs, challenges, and priorities of diverse sub-communities beyond HIV/STIs. To address this, we collaborated with 19 local LGBTQ+ health and rights experts in a two-stage co-creation workshop to identify and prioritise key health issues and to develop a community-driven research agenda. Using thematic analysis, we identified 15 health priorities in Stage 1 and 31 research priorities in Stage 2. Findings from Stage 1 indicated that, despite variations in healthcare needs across sub-communities, participants consistently identified common systemic challenges. These included limited access to LGBTQ+-affirming healthcare services, pervasive prejudice and discrimination, and insufficient capacity within the healthcare system. In Stage 2, the identified research priorities were organised into six overarching domains to address existing gaps: community health needs, health sector capacity, emerging risks, quality of current services, current policy, and LGBTQ+ history and lived experiences. These community-driven health and research priorities can strengthen grassroots advocacy and inform future research, interventions, policy, and funding initiatives focused on LGBTQ+ health in Mongolia.

articles.title: The Harder the State Hits: Coercive Intensity, Protest Repression, and Terrorist Backlash
articles.abstract: Why does repression sometimes deter dissent but at other times generate terrorist backlash? Existing theories emphasise political opportunity, grievances, legitimacy and strategic interaction, but they do not fully explain when repression against dissent escalates into terrorist violence. I argue that backlash depends on the coercive mode through which state force is organised and interpreted. Coercion is more likely to deter when it remains proportionate, fair and predictable. Backlash becomes more likely when coercion creates two interconnected expectation gaps. The first is instrumental: compliance no longer appears sufficient to guarantee safety. The second is normative: the state violates its own claim to exercise force within bounded limits, signalling the exclusion of some groups rather than their protection. Under these conditions, excessive, arbitrary, indiscriminate or visibly disproportionate coercion weakens deterrence while intensifying grievance. It changes the moral ecology through which dissent is interpreted, making terrorist retaliation appear more strategically plausible or morally defensible. I test this argument using country-quarter event data from ACLED and GTD for 2018–2021. The results show that coercive military involvement in domestic security is associated with higher subsequent terrorist activity. The association follows a coercion-intensity gradient: excessive force and military protest repression are more strongly associated with subsequent terrorism than lower-intensity intervention or police-led repression. These findings hold across alternative controls, temporal diagnostics and balancing checks, but remain observational. They suggest that repression backfires not simply because it is forceful, but because some forms of coercion undermine the legitimacy and predictability on which deterrence depends.

articles.title: Collections grow by volume but diversify by exception: donor biographies in MoMA's gift channel, 1929-2024
articles.abstract: A museum's permanent collection is publicly narrated in the first person plural, as choices the institution made. In its gift channel, though, the practical actor is often a single donor handing over a coordinated block of works at once. We read nearly a century of MoMA's donor-identified gift records (1929--2024) as a sequence of donor biographies and ask what each named donor actually changed about whom the collection represents. The recurring surprise is that volume and influence come apart. The donors who give the most works tend to leave the collection's gender and geographic mix where they found it, while the donors who move that mix do so through rare single-year mass gifts rather than a lifetime of collecting. From the six biographies we read most closely we draw a small vocabulary of donor logics, offered as a tool museums can use to read their own gift channels.

articles.title: A Systematic Review of the Effects of Binge Drinking on University Life: Academic, Social, and Behavioral Implications
articles.abstract: Binge drinking is a major public health issue widely associated with university life, particularly during the transition to independent living. High-intensity alcohol consumption among university students is linked to serious physiological, psychological, academic, and social consequences, including neurodegeneration, sleep disturbances, emotional distress, impaired attendance, and risky behaviors. However, existing research remains fragmented across academic, social, and behavioral domains, limiting a holistic understanding of its impact on university life. Additionally, the field relies heavily on cross-sectional studies and self-reported data, making it difficult to establish causal relationships between psychological distress and alcohol misuse. This systematic review aims to analyze the effects of binge drinking on university life by examining its influence on academic performance, social interactions, and maladaptive behavioral patterns. Following the PRISMA protocol, peer-reviewed articles published between 2020 and 2026 from databases such as Scopus and Sage Journals were reviewed. Ten contemporary studies and foundational literature were synthesized. Findings support the Self-Medication Hypothesis, showing that students often use alcohol to cope with ADHD symptoms, loneliness, and emotional distress. The review also identified academic impairment perceptions, changing gender patterns, and the need for longitudinal research and evidence-based university interventions.

articles.title: Short-term rental accommodation: models, impacts and policy responses
articles.authors: Nicole Gurran, Caitlin Buckle, Laurence Troy, Zahra Nasreen, Laura Crommelin, Peter Phibbs, Pranita Shrestha, Declan Redmond, Jason Harrison, Megan Robinson
articles.abstract: This research investigates a range of short-term rental accommodation (STRA) models across Australia and their impacts on housing markets and communities. It identifies the policy responses most likely to resolve the competing demands for short- and long-term rental housing. Many communities are concerned about the housing market and neighbourhood impacts of STRA. Platforms like Airbnb have increased demand for residential property and enabled owners to finance housing investment in new ways. Understanding the range of STRA models, provider motivations, and impacts in different metropolitan and regional settings is key to effective policy development and regulation.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Criterios para el uso transparente, ético y responsable de la inteligencia artificial en publicaciones científicas (CRITERIA)
articles.authors: Lluís Codina, Pere Freixa, Simón Peña-Fernández, carlos Lopezosa
articles.abstract: CRITERIA propone un marco de referencia para orientar a las revistas científicas en la elaboración de políticas sobre el uso transparente, ético y responsable de la inteligencia artificial en la investigación y en la publicación académica. El documento parte de una premisa doble: por un lado, la IA puede aportar eficiencia y apoyo en tareas de ideación, diseño, análisis, síntesis y mejora textual; por otro, puede introducir riesgos graves para la integridad científica, entre ellos plagio, referencias inventadas, datos fabricados, errores factuales, sesgos y erosión del pensamiento crítico. La propuesta se articula en torno a cinco puntos cardinales: la IA no puede ser acreditada como autora, la responsabilidad recae íntegramente en las personas autoras, todo uso de IA exige supervisión crítica humana, el manuscrito debe conservar la voz y la unidad de estilo del autor, y los usos sustanciales deben declararse con transparencia y trazabilidad. Sobre esta base, CRITERIA distingue entre usos permitidos con declaración obligatoria, usos instrumentales no declarables y usos no permitidos, subrayando que la declaración no legitima prácticas contrarias a la integridad académica. Además, ofrece criterios operativos para declarar usos sustanciales, aplicar un test de sustancialidad y evitar la fatiga y el estigma de declaración.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Beyond Description: Causal Inference for Life-Course Trajectories Using Sequence Analysis and G-Computation
articles.abstract: Life-course research asks how employment and family trajectories shape long-run outcomes, yet most empirical studies rely on a cluster-then-regress strategy that cannot recover causal effects when treatment and covariates evolve jointly over time. I propose a two-stage framework that integrates sequence analysis with g-computation. In the first stage, multi-channel sequence analysis and clustering identify empirically prevalent trajectory types whose medoids serve as counterfactual interventions for which positivity is empirically defensible. In the second stage, the causal effect of each medoid trajectory is estimated via g-computation with forward simulation of time-varying covariates using SuperLearner. A semi-synthetic simulation calibrated to the NLSY79 reveals a clear estimator hierarchy: cluster-adjusted regression recovers only a small fraction of the true causal effect, while flexible g-computation recovers the true effects most accurately. An empirical application to NLSY79 data reveals substantial wealth effects of sustained full-time employment and stable family formation. The framework offers life-course researchers a practical approach for defining trajectory interventions and estimating their causal effects.

articles.title: “Just to keep my thoughts good”: Emotion management and ideological reinforcement among mothers on Facebook
articles.abstract: While the psychological and emotional impacts of “intensive mothering” are well documented, we know less about the technological strategies contemporary mothers use to cope with these anxieties as they try to “do it right”. Drawing on qualitative data collected through 18 months of digital ethnographic research, including observation and interviews with 29 mothers, I argue that mothers engage in digitally-mediated emotion management by strategically using Facebook to alleviate their anxieties. In joining groups that make them feel good and leaving groups that make them feel bad, mothers “orchestrate” their Facebook experience in order to maximize positive feelings. I argue that this strategy brings mothers’ emotions into alignment with cultural expectations about how mothering "ought" to feel, while also contributing to ideological reinforcement around what it means to mother “correctly”.

articles.title: From Sector to Access Ecosystem: A Seven-Layer Frame for Online-Only Research on the Digital Class
articles.abstract: This article proposes a seven-layer organizational frame for online-only research on the digital class in state-entangled digital states — contexts in which conventional methods (interviews, representative surveys, labor-market statistics) are either unavailable or systematically biased, and where the sectoral optics of “IT firms plus workers” cannot hold the simultaneous weight of state, regulatory, investment, educational, symbolic-mediation, and civic-audit layers. Conceptually, the article proposes a shift from “sector” to “access-conversion ecosystem”: technical capital is converted into product, infrastructural, financial, educational, symbolic, and accountability resources through seven conversion functions (L1–L7). Methodologically, it proposes an online-only sampling frame with an evidence-ladder discipline (SRC/TRC/EVD/LNK/SNP/AMB registers and EV_STRONG/CONF_3 grades) and an explicitly tagged bias-correction supplement that turns visibility bias from a methodological limitation into a coded variable and a diagnostic instrument for the sociology of online visibility. The Kazakhstan case (Pilot 1: 150 core organizations across the seven layers, with 30 bias-correction supplement candidates) reveals four structural observations: the production core is less directly observable than its public periphery; gatekeeping (L4) is asymmetrically centralized; the education pipeline (L5) operates as a promise machine with a gap between the visibility of promise and the visibility of outcome; symbolic mediation (L6) and civic audit (L7) function as independent capitals, irreducible to “industry plus context”. The “visibility ≠ influence” discipline emerges not as a methodological caveat but as substantive defense of mapped actors against the forms of political miscodification typical of state-entangled contexts. The instrument is applicable to parallel digital states with active ecosystem policy and a developed public-visibility infrastructure.

articles.title: Natural resource curse versus Dutch disease: the explanatory primacy of New Developmentalism over the paradox of plenty
articles.abstract: This article reevaluates the two main explanatory approaches to the paradox of plenty: natural resource curse and Dutch disease. It is argued that the neo-institutionalism literature, while relevant for identifying political pathologies and rent capture incentives, operates predominantly at the level of effects rather than causes. The neoclassical model of Dutch disease, in turn, is limited to temporary price shocks. The article contends that Dutch disease, reformulated by New Developmentalism as an endogenous and chronic market coordination failure − anchored in Ricardian rents and multiple exchange rate equilibria − constitutes the structuring mechanism. The article proposes and formalizes a typology of models within the natural resource curse and Dutch disease theories.

articles.title: A INTERPRETAÇÃO DO DESENVOLVIMENTO DEPENDENTE E ASSOCIADO DE FERNANDO HENRIQUE CARDOSO: UMA REVISITAÇÃO CRÍTICA
articles.abstract: O artigo tem como escopo de revisitar a interpretação sociológica do desenvolvimento dependente e associado da América Latina para Fernando Henrique Cardoso. A hipótese elencada pela pesquisa é de que Cardoso faz uma leitura equivocada sobre desenvolvimento dependente e associado via poupança externa, crendo que o transbordamento de capital forte oriundo das economias centrais seria capaz de alavancar a sofisticação da estrutura produtiva brasileira. O artigo empregou como procedimentos metodológico revisão bibliográfica do autor pesquisado, de renomados pesquisadores que se dedicam a temática e dados empíricos da realidade econômica contemporânea do Brasil. O texto foi dividido em duas seções, além da introdução e considerações finais. Na primeira seção, apresentam-se os principais preceitos da interpretação sociológica da dependência e desenvolvimento de Cardoso. Na segunda seção, demonstra-se como que os preceitos de desenvolvimento dependente e associado de Cardoso levaram a estrutura produtiva brasileira a desnacionalização, a desindustrialização e a reprimarização. O resultado que o texto chega é de que embora a interpretação da dependência e desenvolvimento de Cardoso seja uma abordagem original e intricada, o autor falha ao empregar uma metodologia hipotética e dedutiva, menosprezando aspectos concretos e indutivos da relação econômica centro-periferia, presumindo que seria possível o desenvolvimento econômico dos países periféricos via poupança externa.

articles.title: Revisitando o conceito de Desenvolvimento Econômico e a Escola de Pensamento do Desenvolvimentismo na Economia
articles.abstract: O presente trabalho tem como objetivo revisitar e sistematizar os conceitos de Desenvolvimento Econômico e o de Desenvolvimentismo. A hipótese levantada pela pesquisa é a de que em razão da processualidade histórico-social das Ciências Econômicas, os termos Desenvolvimento Econômico e Desenvolvimentismo podem ser categorizados de forma distinta por opção epistemológica e didática na atualidade. O artigo optou por realizar um estudo bibliográfico. Os procedimentos metodológicos para selecionar as referências foram pesquisa bibliográfica focada nos autores com análise histórica, concreta e indutiva da economia e do fenômeno de desenvolvimento econômico. Os resultados obtidos são de que os termos Desenvolvimento Econômico e Desenvolvimentismo podem ser categorizados de forma distinta, o primeiro como conceito empregado por diversas escolas do pensamento econômico; o segundo é uma Escola de Pensamento Econômico em si e que possui quatro correntes teóricas (Desenvolvimentismo Clássico, Social Desenvolvimentismo, Novo Desenvolvimentismo, Desenvolvimentismo Socialista de Mercado).

articles.title: Cultura do privilégio: entrave para o desenvolvimento econômico na América Latina
articles.abstract: O presente artigo tem como objetivo contribuir para o entendimento dos entraves para um projeto de desenvolvimento econômico na América Latina contemporânea. A premissa elencada pela pesquisa é a de que os projetos e as estratégias desenvolvimentistas executados pelos governos vanguardistas nos países da América Latina sofrem de entraves e se tornam truncados em virtude, frequentemente, da cultura do privilégio presente na sociedade latino-americana. O artigo optou em realizar pesquisa de revisão bibliográfica pautada em leitura sintópica. Os resultados obtidos são de que, na América Latina, grande parte da elite dirigente carrega introjetada um desprezo pelo seu próprio povo –visto como “subgente”– e um fascínio e subalternidade perante o colonizador –economias centrais– visto como modelos de humanos e de civilização. Isso faz com que haja uma cultura do privilégio responsável por um horror social e fator relevante pelo entrave ao desenvolvimento econômico na região.

articles.title: COOPERAÇÃO BRASIL – ÁFRICA EM BIOCOMBUSTÍVEIS DURANTE O GOVERNO LULA: UMA PARCERIA PARA O DESENVOLVIMENTO
articles.authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Beatriz Rodrigues Bessa Mattos
articles.abstract: O artigo propõe realizar uma análise sobre a cooperação no setor de biocombustíveis entre Brasil e África durante o governo do Presidente Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva (2003 – 2010). O estudo procurou, ademais, elencar os principais projetos, tratados e memorandos assinados pelo Brasil, visando à promoção dos combustíveis renováveis na África.

articles.title: CHINA-AFRICA RELATIONS IN POLITICAL ECONOMY OF THE WORLD-SYSTEM: IN BETWEEN EXCLUDING-INSERTION AND INCLUDING-INSERTION
articles.authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Mônica Heinzelmann Portella de Aguiar
articles.abstract: The purpose of this article is to investigate how the current China-Africa relations integrate the Political Economy of the World-System. The paper starts by explaining the theoretical framework of the research, the World-systems theory. Upon examination of the current economic and trade relationship between China and Africa, the research assessed two different models that the underdeveloped economies integrate the international economy: the excluding-insertion and the including-insertion. The article used the descriptive-qualitative methodology, using primary and secondary sources. The results indicate that China, when dealing with Africa, is reproducing exploitative power behaviour, the same way all nations are or have been part of the "leader circle" of the world-system have done previously. Keywords: Political Economic of the World-System; Economic development; International Political Economy; Chinese Foreign Policy; Interstate relations China-Africa.

articles.title: When Cumulative Advantage Inverts: Within-Canon Stratification in Korean Modern Art, 1929-2002
articles.abstract: In the first decade of an artist's career, dense ties to a trusted circle of galleries and juries protect against stagnation. After a decade, the same ties begin to constrain, and the cost intensifies with cumulative recognition. The cumulative-advantage tradition predicts the opposite: that within an already-consecrated elite, the most-endorsed members should be the best insulated. This paper documents the regularity that runs the other way. Among 495 modern Korean artists assembled by the DA-Arts oral-history project in 2000, a canon spanning 1929 to 2002 and 16,424 institutional encounters, the protective value of dense ties is non-stationary across career time. The least-validated members of the canon show no penalty for repeated engagement with the same institutional partners; the most-validated face the steepest. This is a within-canon Matthew-inversion, not a field-level reversal of cumulative advantage. Because the within-canon recognition axis is built from the same evaluative apparatus the analysis treats as the outcome, the gradient documents how a consecration apparatus distributes its own outputs at the top of its own scale, not an independent recognition signal. The paper develops one candidate interpretation, evaluative redundancy, the diminishing informational return of repeated endorsement from the same gatekeeper. A ritualization reading is observationally equivalent on these data, and the paper leaves it empirically open. Whether the same within-canon Matthew-inversion recurs in other consecrated populations, from the Hollywood A-list to elite journalists, is the natural next test.

articles.title: O PENSAMENTO DA CEPAL DE 2010 A 2018: O ENFOQUE NA MUDANÇA ESTRUTURAL PRODUTIVA PARA IGUALDADE (MEPI)
articles.authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Hermano Caixeta Ibrahim, Leandro Pereira Morais
articles.abstract: O artigo tem como unidade de análise a Comissão Econômica para América Latina e Caribe das Nações Unidas (CEPAL/ONU). O objetivo é de investigar a atual fase de seu pensamento com forte enfoque na igualdade. A pesquisa categoriza o pensamento cepalino em duas fases distintas: a primeira, do Estado-empreendedor e método histórico-estruturalista (1948-1990), e a segunda, do Estado-gerencial e método histórico-neoestruturalista (1990-atualidade). A hipótese elencada é de que o pensamento cepalino desde 2010 se afastou da proposta de Transformação Produtiva com Equidade (TPE) – defendido, inicialmente, pelo Neoestruturalismo – e está estabelecendo uma nova concepção: Mudança Estrutural Produtiva para Igualdade (MEPI) A pesquisa optou em priorizar como procedimentos técnicos uma abordagem sistemática por meio da avaliação crítica dos dados bibliográficos e documentais sobre a temática, sobretudo os próprios documentos produzidos pela CEPAL.

articles.title: A importância da Economia Social e Solidária (ESS) para as propostas da Cepal na atualidade
articles.authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Leandro Pereira Morais, Miguel Juan Bacic, Daniel Francisco Nagao Menezes
articles.abstract: O artigo correlaciona os estudos e os autores da Economia Solidária e Social com a nova agenda de pesquisa da Cepal, estabelecendo pontos de contato, especialmente, com a Agenda Regional de Desenvolvimento Social Inclusivo e com a Mudança Estrutural Produtiva para Igualdade na América Latina. Os procedimentos técnico-metodológico foram: abordagem sistemática por meio da avaliação dos dados bibliográficos e documentais da Cepal e o campo acadêmico da ESS. Os resultados revelaram que a Cepal teria capacidade técnica, humana e material para contribuir com os avanços dos estudos da ESS, quantificando, qualificando e potencializando uma eficiência social no desenvolvimento econômico latino-americano.

articles.title: Capitalismo agrário e movimentos campesinos no Paraguai
articles.authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Fernando Antonio da Costa Vieira
articles.abstract: O artigo busca analisar, por meio de levantamento bibliográfico e documental, o desenvolvimento do capitalismo rural e os movimentos sociais campesinos no Paraguai. Primeiramente, a pesquisa procura abordar a matriz empresarial-monopolista-agroexportadora de ocupação e de exploração da terra no Paraguai, com a significativa influência de ruralistas brasileiros, em detrimento da matriz familiar-cooperativista-alimentícia. Na segunda parte, empenha-se em apresentar os principais movimentos sociais campesinos do país, seus embates e meios de atuação. Por fim, demonstra a importância destes últimos para a democratização da terra, a soberania e a segurança alimentar paraguaia.

articles.title: Relações Brasil-China: uma parceria para o desenvolvimento ou uma relação de codependência?
articles.authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Mônica Heinzelmann Portella de Aguiar, Carlos Eduardo Tauil
articles.abstract: Desde 2000, as relações sino-brasileiras vêm se aprofundando ao ponto da China ter se tornado o maior parceiro comercial do Brasil. Este artigo visa analisar a progressão destas relações econômicas e políticas a fim de compreender qual o impacto que a ascensão recente da China no contexto internacional teve sobre o desenvolvimento brasileiro. Dentro do referencial da Teoria da Dependência, a pesquisa levanta a pregunta se a parceria com a China contribuiu para o efetivo desenvolvimento brasileiro ou se foi criada uma nova forma de dependência. A primeira parte da pesquisa aborda a Teoria da Dependência e sua interpretação das relações entre países periféricos e centrais no complexo sistema internacional. A segunda parte apresenta um breve histórico das relações sino-brasileiras e analisa o seu atual estágio. A pesquisa propõe um novo conceito para interpretar as relações entre esses dois países semiperiféricos: o de codependência. Como procedimento técnico-metodológicos, a pesquisa utilizou de fontes primárias e secundárias, como indicadores econômicos, produção bibliográfica e documental.

articles.title: CRISE DEMOCRÁTICA E AS PROPOSIÇÕES DO MUNICIPALISMO LIBERTÁRIO DE MURRAY BOOKCHIN E DO RADICALISMO DEMOCRÁTICO DE CHANTAL MOUFFE
articles.abstract: O presente trabalho tem como objetivo apresentar e comparar os escritos de Murray Bookchin e sua Teoria de Municipalismo Libertário e os de Chantal Mouffe com seu Radicalismo Democrático. Ambos, pouco estudados no Brasil, têm seus escritos marcados pela busca de uma democracia mais radical e plural. A hipótese que o texto levanta é a de que enquanto o pensamento de Bookchin visa não somente a radicalização da democracia, mas a adoção do próprio método democrático em sua forma mais pura como mecanismo de superação do sistema capitalista; as prescrições de Mouffe se mantêm reformista e de certo modo flertando com o liberalismo, a despeito de utilizar um campo semântico e conceitual socialista. A apresentação dos autores e de seus pensamentos respeitou a ordem cronológica na qual cada um produziu seus trabalhos, Bookchin na década de 1960 e Mouffe na década de 1990. Assim, na primeira parte dedicou-se a explanar sobre o Municipalismo Libertário, na segunda parte sobre o Radicalismo Democrático e nas considerações finais o trabalho intentou em fazer um balanço entre as duas teorias.

articles.title: Análise do Discurso e o uso dos meios de comunicação na Política Externa de Boa Vizinhança
articles.abstract: O presente artigo, fazendo uso da Análise do Discurso, buscou lançar um olhar na utilização e nos impactos causados pelos meios de comunicação, no Brasil, na Política Externa de Boa Vizinhança do governo de Franklin Delano Roosevelt, presidente dos Estados Unidos da América – EUA de 1933 a 1945. Primeiramente, o estudo procurou traçar um panorama sobre os preceitos essenciais da Análise do Discurso, valendo-se, mormente, dos estudos de Michel Foucault. Posteriormente, foi realizado um breve levantamento bibliográfico e histórico, de fontes primárias e secundárias, com vistas a apresentar a produção discursiva da Política Externa de Boa Vizinhança e suas principais diretrizes para a América Latina, sobretudo para o Brasil. O trabalho inferiu, por fim, que os aspectos culturais produzidos pela Política Externa de Boa Vizinhança e vendidos aos demais países latino-americanos divulgaram estilos de vida, conceitos, visões e foi uma das primeiras exportações de versões idealizadas do American way of Life.

articles.title: A macroeconomia do Social Desenvolvimentismo: um estudo da Rede Desenvolvimentista
articles.abstract: O presente artigo tem como unidade de análise o Social Desenvolvimentismo. A categoria de análise é sua macroeconomia. O estudo adotou como procedimento metodológico a abordagem sistemática por meio da seleção, do fichamento e da avaliação crítica de dados bibliográficos que formam o arcabouço teórico do Social Desenvolvimentismo, sobretudo os estudos da Rede Desenvolvimentista (Rede D). O artigo foi dividido em duas seções; a primeira apresenta uma breve história da edificação da escola e da operacionalização conceitual do Social Desenvolvimentismo; a segunda seção concentra-se em elucidar a macroeconomia do Social Desenvolvimento. Os resultados da pesquisa são que o Social Desenvolvimentismo resgata o caráter político na macroeconomia ao defender um modelo de política e de regime macroeconômico mais adaptável e menos engessado, no entanto sem enveredar para “aventuras” ou “populismos”. O modelo, assim, contribui para o amadurecimento do debate desenvolvimentista no Brasil atualmente.

articles.title: A IMIGRAÇÃO HAITIANA PARA O BRASIL: CAUSAS E DESAFIOS
articles.authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Carlos Alberto Alencar de Andrade, Beatriz Rodrigues Bessa Mattos
articles.abstract: O artigo procura analisar a recente imigração haitiana para o Brasil. O texto utiliza-se de conceitos como visto humanitário e refugio. Embasa-se em autores da Teoria Histórico-Estruturalista para compreender os fatores de atração e repulsão presente nos fluxos migratórios. O estudo, por fim, busca averiguar as ações e a estrutura político-administrativa brasileira para lidar com os imigrantes.

articles.title: A Reproductive Health and Mortality Gradient for Women, Mothers, and Girls in Non-Kin Out-of-Home Placement Evidence, Mechanism, and the Federal Disability Framework
articles.abstract: Background. Out-of-home care (OOHC), defined as the removal of a child from a parent into kinship, non-kin foster, or congregate placement, is one of the largest non-medical interventions applied to women and girls over the life course. The evidence base for the general adult-mortality effect of OOHC is now mature. A six-country meta-analysis covering 3.2 million individuals reports a pooled all-cause adult mortality hazard ratio of 2.21 (95% CI 1.62 to 3.02), with tripled suicide risk. The sexual-health literature has not yet developed the female-specific reproductive-health arc that runs through this mortality signal, nor the parallel mortality burden carried by mothers whose children are taken into care. Objectives. The paper has three. The first is to synthesise the strongest causal-design evidence linking OOHC exposure as a child, and child removal as a mother, to female-specific reproductive-health endpoints, namely accelerated pubertal timing, sexual victimisation and exploitation in placement, sexually transmitted infection (STI) and HIV burden, adolescent pregnancy, obstetric morbidity, and maternal mortality. The second is to locate this evidence within the developmental and HPA-axis biological framework that links childhood adversity to reproductive harm. The third is to place the harm within the federal disability framework that already designates reproductive function as a protected major bodily function under the Americans with Disabilities Act Amendments Act of 2008 and HHS Section 504. Findings. Multiple convergent lines of evidence document a graded female-specific reproductive-health and mortality burden. The lines include the instrumental-variable design (Doyle 2007), two discordant-sibling register cohorts (Brännström 2020; Wall-Wieler 2018), a 50-year longitudinal cohort (Sacker 2021), four meta-analytic syntheses (Batty 2022; Hughes 2017; Mendle 2019; Zhang 2023), and two large quasi-experimental administrative cohorts from Manitoba (Brownell 2024; Kenny 2026). Girls in OOHC experience earlier pubertal onset, four-fold elevated lifetime sexual-abuse risk relative to non-OOHC peers, two- to three-fold higher rates of adolescent pregnancy, elevated laboratory-confirmed STI prevalence, and disproportionate representation among commercial sexual exploitation cases. Manitoba register evidence demonstrates that mothers whose children are removed by child protective services face all-cause mortality 3 to 4 times above peers, with deaths concentrated in suicide, overdose, and homicide. The biological mechanism is consistent across studies and includes dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal and hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axes, chronic inflammation, prenatal cortisol programming, and 11β-HSD2 suppression.

articles.title: От IT-сектора к экосистеме доступа: семислойная рамка online-only исследования цифрового класса
articles.abstract: This article proposes a seven-layer organizational frame for online-only research on the digital class in state-entangled digital states — contexts in which conventional methods (interviews, representative surveys, labor-market statistics) are either unavailable or systematically biased, and where the sectoral optics of “IT firms plus workers” cannot hold the simultaneous weight of state, regulatory, investment, educational, symbolic-mediation, and civic-audit layers. Conceptually, the article proposes a shift from “sector” to “access-conversion ecosystem”: technical capital is converted into product, infrastructural, financial, educational, symbolic, and accountability resources through seven conversion functions (L1–L7). Methodologically, it proposes an online-only sampling frame with an evidence-ladder discipline (SRC/TRC/EVD/LNK/SNP/AMB registers and EV_STRONG/CONF_3 grades) and an explicitly tagged bias-correction supplement that turns visibility bias from a methodological limitation into a coded variable and a diagnostic instrument for the sociology of online visibility. The Kazakhstan case (Pilot 1: 150 core organizations across the seven layers, with 30 bias-correction supplement candidates) reveals four structural observations: the production core is less directly observable than its public periphery; gatekeeping (L4) is asymmetrically centralized; the education pipeline (L5) operates as a promise machine with a gap between the visibility of promise and the visibility of outcome; symbolic mediation (L6) and civic audit (L7) function as independent capitals, irreducible to “industry plus context”. The “visibility ≠ influence” discipline emerges not as a methodological caveat but as substantive defense of mapped actors against the forms of political miscodification typical of state-entangled contexts. The instrument is applicable to parallel digital states with active ecosystem policy and a developed public-visibility infrastructure.

articles.title: Digital Diffusion: Mobile Internet and the Spread of Cultural Scripts about Family Life
articles.authors: Casey F. Breen, Ridhi Kashyap, Till Koebe
articles.abstract: Global cultural diffusion theories have long emphasized how institutional actors such as intergovernmental organizations, international non-governmental organizations, and state agencies spread cultural scripts. We theorize that mobile internet constitutes a qualitatively distinct pathway for the diffusion of these scripts, shifting exposure from institutionally mediated and episodic to more individualized, continuous, and algorithmically curated. This reconfiguration changes both which cultural models individuals encounter and the frequency with which individuals are exposed to them. Using the staggered rollout of mobile internet across Nigeria as an empirical case, we find that expanding coverage reduces fertility, corresponding to a 9% relative decline in the annual probability of birth, consistent with shifts in contraceptive use, ideal family size, and women’s autonomy in financial and healthcare decision-making. These findings suggest that digital infrastructure does not simply extend existing diffusion processes but can reshape them, with measurable consequences for family life.

articles.title: A Multi-District Markov Chain Monte Carlo Sampler for Redistricting
articles.abstract: Random sampling of redistricting plans has become a standard tool for detecting gerrymandering and evaluating proposed maps. Most MCMC redistricting algorithms operate by merging two adjacent districts and splitting them back into two new districts at each step of the chain. I extend this Merge-Split framework with the Multiple Merge Sequential Split (MMSS) algorithm, which generalizes each step to merge ℓ ≥ 2 adjacent districts and repartition them into ℓ new districts via a sequence of ℓ − 1 uniform spanning tree cuts. MMSS targets the same spanning-forest-weighted distribution used by existing samplers, and I derive the Metropolis-Hastings acceptance ratio. I validate the algorithm by comparing samples against exactly enumerated distributions on small grid maps and compare its scalability with two related algorithms on a realistic redistricting problem. The algorithm supports any value of ℓ ≥ 2, giving users direct control over per-step exploration. MMSS is implemented in the open-source redist R package.

articles.title: Limits of Language Models for Qualitative Coding: Detecting Calls to Action in r/Antiwork
articles.abstract: Recent studies have demonstrated that large language models (LLMs) can match human experts’ accuracy in qualitatively coding social media data. However, proprietary LLMs accessed through APIs raise concerns related to cost, accessibility, and data protection. Although smaller language models (SLMs) have received less scholarly attention, they can be deployed locally and are open-access, which in turn can mitigate many of the ethical concerns of LLMs. This study evaluates three open-access SLMs in two zero-shot qualitative coding tasks identifying and classifying calls to action (CTAs) within posts from the Reddit community r/Antiwork. Drawing on framing theory and the concept of infrapolitics, we examine how models interpret both collective and individually oriented forms of mobilizing discourse. Across both tasks, models demonstrated consistently low agreement with human-coded ground truth and were least successful at identifying implicit, individual, and infrapolitical calls to action embedded in personal narrative. Qualitative close reading revealed that models most accurately identified CTAs when posts contained explicit directive language and recognizable collective action framing, but were less able to identify CTAs embedded in community-specific and context-dependent forms of mobilization. These findings suggest that zero-shot prompting may be poorly suited to qualitative coding tasks involving implicit social movement discourse. Furthermore, computational approaches to researching mobilization may systematically privilege explicit collective action over individualized or infrapolitical forms of resistance.

articles.title: Reframing biodiversity conservation using social data and artificial intelligence
articles.authors: Oscar Morton, Sebastian Heilpern, Melissa Arias, Sharon Baruch-Mordo, Lucy Cash, Nathan Fox, Annette Hübschle, Koffi Valentin Mawougnigan, Ting Luo, Jacqueline Jürgens
articles.abstract: Human behaviour is central to biodiversity loss, yet conservation rarely harnesses the wealth of social datasets that capture and reflect these behaviours. Here, we argue that integrating diverse social data with recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) can reveal early behavioural signals of emerging threats, enabling a shift from reactive mitigation to anticipatory action. Using illegal wildlife trade mitigation as an illustrative example, we outline how AI-enabled analyses of social data can be applied across the conservation project lifecycle, and highlight key safeguards and priority steps for piloting these approaches in policy-relevant settings.

articles.title: On the New Design Approach for Mountain Villas
articles.abstract: Based on the value of mountain villas and the problems they face, this paper proposes a new design approach for mountain villas using electric vertical takeoff and landing (eVTOL) aircraft as the primary means of transportation. The advantages of this new type of mountain villa are: 1) No need to construct roads; they can be built on any terrain, even on isolated peaks, allowing residents to stay far away from bustling cities without inconvenience. 2) The scenery and privacy will be of the highest order, offering residents a breathtaking living experience and ultimate enjoyment. These villas will set a new benchmark for luxury estates and are also suitable for high end vacation and leisure development. 3) The environmental impact is within acceptable limits, and at the same time, these villas will add new landscape features to mountainous areas. However, there are also some non negligible issues, such as construction costs, eVTOL traffic safety, seismic and wind resistance of the buildings, and public perception. These issues are discussed in detail.

articles.title: Hans Hass and Jacques-Yves Cousteau. Pioneering Achievements and Structural Inequalities in the Construction of Maritime Memory.
articles.abstract: This study examines the parallel yet highly divergent careers of Hans Hass and Jacques-Yves Cousteau, two central pioneers of twentieth century underwater exploration. While both contributed substantially to marine science, diving technology, and the visual mediation of the underwater world, their long-term global recognition developed in markedly different ways. Drawing on diverse interdisciplinary perspectives, the article explores how geopolitical conditions, institutional support systems, narrative self-presentation, and the rise of a global media ecology shaped their public visibility. Beyond a biographical or technical comparison, the juxtaposition of Hass and Cousteau is deliberately employed as a conceptual case study to examine the formation of maritime memory in the twentieth century underwater research. The findings demonstrate that Cousteau’s worldwide prominence resulted not solely from scientific or technological achievement, but from his exceptional integration into powerful institutional, technological, and cultural infrastructures that amplified and internationalized his work. In contrast, Hass—despite significant early innovation and ecological awareness—was constrained by weaker institutional embeddedness, a scientifically restrained and emotionally neutral serial television aesthetic with limited long-term repeatability, and subsequent theoretical isolation. A decisive factor in this divergence was Cousteau’s entrepreneurial engagement with emerging television markets, which transformed underwater exploration into a scalable media enterprise and provided the structural basis for his enduring visibility. Overall, the analysis suggests that maritime memory is shaped less by scientific merit alone than by the ways media, politics, and economic scalability stabilize certain cultural narratives. These dynamics have produced asymmetrical legacies that continue to shape how ocean exploration is remembered and imagined today.

articles.title: Locally-Driven Developmental United Front Work: County-Level Mobilization in China's Rural Revitalization
articles.abstract: This article develops the concept of "locally-driven developmental united front work" to explain how county-level united front departments in China have moved beyond traditional political coordination and elite co-optation toward active involvement in economic growth, cultural tourism, and social governance. Drawing on participant observation and archival analysis of Shangyou County (Jiangxi Province) between 2022 and 2025, the study identifies four mechanisms through which united front resources are transformed into developmental outcomes: resource capitalization, platform repurposing, institutional bricolage, and cross-sector brokerage. The analysis focuses on five empirical domains: cultural tourism integration, private-sector innovation, non-Party cadre cultivation, grassroots organizational innovation, and digital capacity building. The findings suggest that under conditions of fiscal constraint and strong upper-level policy support, county-level united front leaders can function as developmental entrepreneurs who recombine existing institutional materials to generate locally tailored growth strategies. This reconceptualization extends the literature on China’s united front system and contributes to broader debates on local state developmentalism and institutional entrepreneurship.

articles.title: Governance causality theory: A causal architecture for regulatory and administrative systems
articles.abstract: This theoretical manuscript develops a causal architecture for governance and administrative systems, explaining how authority, regulation, and institutional design interact to produce administrative outcomes. It forms part of a thesis‑by‑publication on governance architecture and delegated authority. The paper proposes a structural model linking causal mechanisms of rule formation, execution‑time authority, and institutional adaptation within public administration.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Disentangling Repetition and Imitation Components of Preferential Attachment in Social Action Networks
articles.abstract: Models of social action networks frequently find that preferential attachment -- the recurring pattern in which individuals or entities that already have many social ties tend to attract even more over time -- is a powerful predictor of social interaction over time. The most common explanation of preferential attachment is social imitation, i.e., selecting interaction targets based on how others have selected targets in the past. However, the most commonly used measure of preferential attachment, target indegree, conflates two distinct mechanisms: social imitation, in which actors mimic behaviors they observe in others, and self-repetition, in which actors repeat their own past behaviors. Drawing from classical sociological theory, this paper proposes a conceptual and empirical distinction between these processes and demonstrates that they exhibit different sensitivities to features of the interaction environment. Using a hierarchical relational event model across a corpus of hundreds of social action networks, we show that models separating imitation and repetition provide improved fit and more interpretable results. In addition, while social imitation varies systematically with interaction visibility in the social ecology, repetition remains significant and positive when interaction visibility is low. The findings clarify the behavioral foundations of preferential attachment and provide conceptual precision in modeling sequential social dynamics across social ecologies.

articles.title: Toward a Generative Theory of Social Structure: How Social Feedback and Reputation Produce Norms, Power, and Meaning
articles.abstract: How do behavioral norms stabilize, shared interpretive frameworks consolidate, and power hierarchies form and reproduce — all without central coordination, prior agreement, or external enforcement, and all from the ordinary dynamics of everyday interaction? This paper presents a generative answer: a single recursive feedback mechanism, formalized as the socially responsive agent (SRA) model, from which all of these structures emerge endogenously, without external enforcement, pre-existing normative orders, or tradition-specific auxiliary assumptions. The mechanism is compact. Agents monitor symbolically mediated social feedback and adapt their behavior to increase their reputational standing within a reference group. The iterated operation of this loop, extended by gossip networks and imitation of opinion leaders, generates behavioral norms, shared schemas, and stable power hierarchies as natural consequences rather than as independent phenomena. These structures are unified within the concept of the social reward economy, which produces cohesion, polarization, resistance, and manipulation as structural outcomes of the same feedback dynamics. The theoretical stakes extend further. Mapped onto the SRA mechanism, the core contributions of Mead, Giddens, Bourdieu, Habermas, Luhmann, and Coleman appear not as incommensurable rivals but as accounts of different components of the same generative process. Most consequentially, the Habermas–Luhmann debate — long treated as an irresolvable confrontation between incompatible social ontologies — turns out to describe two empirically distinct regimes of the same underlying mechanism: one prevailing before opinion leadership consolidates, one after. What appeared philosophical becomes an empirical question. The paper develops this argument as a unified whole, drawing on work developed in its companion papers.

articles.title: Healing Beyond the Table: The Role of Trauma and Gender in Mediation
articles.abstract: Peace negotiations frequently collapse not because of failed diplomacy, but because of unaddressed trauma and the systematic exclusion of women. This policy analysis examines two critical dimensions of sustainable peacebuilding — trauma-informed practice and gender-inclusive participation — through comparative case studies of Rwanda and South Sudan. Rwanda's Gacaca courts illustrate both the healing potential and the re-traumatization risks of community-based truth-telling, while South Sudan's repeated peace failures expose the costs of excluding women and ignoring psychosocial wounds. Drawing on the Women, Peace and Security (WPS) framework anchored in UN Security Council Resolution 1325, this piece argues that durable peace requires mediation processes that are simultaneously trauma-conscious and gender-sensitive. Five concrete recommendations are offered for mediators, policymakers, and international organizations.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Hardwarism and biofoundry imaginaries in South Korea
articles.abstract: International proponent literature legitimates biofoundries (the automated infrastructure of synthetic biology) through democratization rhetoric. That register has bifurcated since 2015 and faced a parallel strategic-asset register since 2022. How Korean stakeholders who shape biofoundry governance position themselves in this fragmenting register has not been measured. A fifteen-expert Korean panel evaluated four candidate framings against an anchoring interview with a senior researcher at a national biofoundry centre. A four-corpus parallel inspection (Korean policy texts, international policy and proponent-community documents, outward-facing Korean publications, civic-bioethics archive material across three sub-strata) and a Singapore illustrative vignette converge on the panel finding. Among the four offered framings, eight of fifteen mark bio-democratization most problematic while eleven endorse the instrumentalist bio-factory framing as most useful. The democratization lexicon appears in Korean civic-society discourse but disappears in statutory and biofoundry-policy registers, and every panelist-articulated alternative preserves the producer-consumer schema rather than the co-producer alternative. We read the pattern as consistent with Kim's (2011) hardwarism, the Korean discursive structure that treats technology as hardware produced by experts and adopted by users, a reading licensed for cross-national testing rather than within-panel proof. The Korean case sits in a developmental-state pathway, distinct from the UK pathway of attenuated democratization framings and from the post-2022 international pivot to strategic-asset framing. The paper contributes a within-group check protocol for stakeholder-consultation design and a policy diagnosis. Democratization vocabulary will not deliver the participatory legitimacy it promises, whereas an accountability-and-interoperability vocabulary aligned with the bio-factory framing does more analytical work.

articles.title: Systematic Evaluation of Nation-State Propaganda in LLM Outputs
articles.authors: Kevin T. Greene, Jacob N Shapiro
articles.abstract: The rapid growth and usability of AI is creating new risks to information quality, including the possibility that chatbot responses spread propaganda from state-backed influence campaigns. There is little systematic evidence regarding how frequently LLMs spread such narratives when responding to user queries. Previous attempts to assess these risks rely on small numbers of evaluation questions, use opaque query construction procedures, and fail to account for key sources of uncertainty in model responses. We address these gaps with an automated, reproducible pipeline that samples documented state-backed propaganda and news items, programmatically generates evaluation questions, and audits LLM-generated responses. We evaluate whether five prominent models: (1) reproduce arguments aligned with documented Kremlin propaganda narratives; (2) present the Kremlin perspective on events without providing corrective context; and (3) cite known propaganda outlets. We find substantially lower rates of propaganda-aligned responses and propaganda-source citation than prior small-sample work would suggest. These risks are particularly rare for questions resembling ordinary information searches about Ukraine. Our approach enables systematic comparisons across models and issue areas, supports ongoing monitoring, and could be extended to many other hotly-debated topics.

subjects.name: Public Affairs, Public Policy and Public Administration

articles.title: Navigating the Fractured Frontier: Legal Risks in Vietnam’s International Financial Centers and Strategic Pathways for Global and Vietnam Investors
articles.abstract: Vietnam’s International Financial Centers (IFCs) in Ho Chi Minh City and Danang represent a strategic pivot toward deep integration into the global financial architecture. Established under Resolution 222/2025/QH15, these centers promise flexible legal frameworks, preferential tax regimes, and specialized dispute resolution mechanisms. Yet, implementation reveals both visible and hidden legal risks that can undermine predictability and investment efficacy. Employing doctrinal legal analysis, comparative institutional benchmarking, and contrarian critique, this paper dissects two risk strata: visible risks (ambiguous legal status, fragmented governance, foreign exchange controls, and AML/CFT gaps) and hidden risks (enforcement discretion, judicial conservatism, cross-border data sovereignty conflicts, and geopolitical friction). Challenging the traditional “legal enclave” paradigm, the study argues that IFC success depends on systemic alignment with Vietnam’s domestic legal ecosystem rather than institutional isolation. The paper proposes a strategic architecture encompassing hybrid dispute resolution, dynamic contracting with stabilization clauses, co-regulatory dialogue, and climate-aligned ESG compliance. Its primary contribution lies in a multi-layered legal risk taxonomy and a paradigm shift from risk mitigation toward adaptive institutional co-creation, offering actionable frameworks for multinational enterprises, legal practitioners, and policymakers navigating emerging market financial hubs.

articles.title: Reconsidering the Relationship Between Intelligence and Right-Wing Authoritarianism and Social Dominance Orientation: Evidence from a Discordant Twin Analysis
articles.authors: Mirko Ruks, Christian Kandler, Jana Instinske, Edward Bell
articles.abstract: Previous research has consistently shown that higher intelligence (IQ) is linked to lower levels of Right-Wing Authoritarianism (RWA) and Social Dominance Orientation (SDO). However, prior studies have been largely correlational, leaving it unclear to what degree these associations are driven by unobserved confounding. This study addresses this gap using longitudinal data from 6144 twins in the German TwinLife survey and employing a discordant-twin design. We estimated latent-variable within--between models, which allowed us to estimate the effect of IQ on RWA and SDO four years later while accounting for unobserved genetic and familial confounding. The pooled results replicate the negative associations reported in prior work, but the within-family estimates using identical twins show that these associations are substantially inflated by unobserved confounding. Specifically, the link between IQ and RWA appears to be partly driven by common genetic influences, whereas the association between IQ and SDO is primarily confounded by shared environmental factors. These findings suggest that the role of cognitive ability in fostering more liberal attitudes is often overstated and underscore the value of genetically informed designs in political psychology.

articles.title: La paradoja del bienestar emprendedor: una revisión narrativa sobre autonomía, estrés y desempeño
articles.abstract: El bienestar emprendedor se ha consolidado como un campo relevante para comprender el emprendimiento más allá de sus resultados económicos. Esta revisión narrativa sintetiza literatura científica publicada hasta el 31 de diciembre de 2023 sobre la relación entre emprendimiento, bienestar psicológico, estrés, desempeño, persistencia y sostenibilidad del negocio. El argumento central es que el emprendimiento produce una paradoja de bienestar: puede ofrecer autonomía, propósito, aprendizaje, identidad y satisfacción, pero también generar incertidumbre financiera, sobrecarga laboral, conflicto vida-trabajo, miedo al fracaso, agotamiento y deterioro de la salud mental. La revisión organiza la literatura en tres partes. Primero, se presentan los fundamentos conceptuales del bienestar emprendedor, integrando bienestar subjetivo, bienestar eudaimónico, salud mental, satisfacción laboral y equilibrio vida-trabajo. Segundo, se analizan factores que fortalecen o deterioran el bienestar, incluyendo autoeficacia, resiliencia, capital psicológico, pasión, apoyo familiar, redes, demandas laborales, género y contexto institucional. Tercero, se examinan las consecuencias del bienestar sobre desempeño, innovación, toma de decisiones, persistencia, salida del negocio, fracaso y recuperación. Se propone un modelo conceptual que entiende el bienestar emprendedor como resultado dinámico de la interacción entre demandas, recursos, mecanismos psicológicos, resultados empresariales y moderadores contextuales. El artículo concluye que el bienestar debe considerarse una dimensión central de la sostenibilidad emprendedora, especialmente en contextos latinoamericanos donde la informalidad, la desigualdad, el emprendimiento por necesidad y la limitada protección social pueden intensificar la vulnerabilidad de quienes emprenden. Palabras clave: bienestar emprendedor; emprendimiento; estrés; salud mental; desempeño; revisión narrativa.

articles.title: Moral Regulation in Sequential Decisions
articles.authors: Umberto Galmarini, Astrid Gamba, Ibai Martínez-Macías
articles.abstract: Do individuals become more generous after harming others, or less generous after doing the right thing? We study whether moral behavior spills over across sequential decisions through moral accounting: individuals may offset prior moral debts through subsequent prosocial behavior (moral cleansing) or draw on prior moral credits to justify lower generosity (moral licensing). In an online experiment, participants first make a fair or unfair allocation in a Dictator Minigame. They then learn whether the Receiver’s payoff was determined by their own choice or by chance, and make an unanticipated decision about whether to donate part of their earnings to a charity. By varying responsibility for realized social outcomes, the design generates different moral states associated with the same first-stage choice, which can trigger compensatory behavior in the subsequent donation decision. We find a sharp asymmetry. After choosing the fair allocation, being responsible for the Receiver’s favorable outcome significantly reduces subsequent donations, consistent with moral licensing. After choosing the unfair allocation, responsibility has no average effect on giving, but this null effect conceals substantial heterogeneity in individual responses. Overall, the results show that responsibility for outcomes can shape later prosocial behavior, but does so asymmetrically across good and bad deeds.

articles.title: Who’s afraid of relativism?
articles.abstract: Speakers on both the political right and left often suggest that their opponents are engaged in, or given aid and comfort by, “relativism” or “postmodernism,” a rejection of universal standards of evidence, reason, or morality. Such rejection is taken to result in a collapse of norms into nihilism, reducing politics to brute propaganda and power play. Drawing on the philosophical tradition of American pragmatism, I argue that “relativism” is a boogeyman fear, and that rejection of the idea of universal standards does not make discourse, argument, or politics any less based in evidence, reason, or morality than they already were. I suggest that both accusations of relativism and their corollary claims of transcendent, universal justification are unproductive, overheated distractions from the difficult and pressing work of democratic deliberation, negotiation, and compromise.

articles.title: 100 ANOS DA REVOLUÇÃO RUSSA: ENSINAMENTOS DA ATUAÇÃO DOS ANARQUISTAS
articles.authors: Isaias Albertin de Moraes, Fernando Antonio da Costa Vieira
articles.abstract: O presente artigo procura apresentar, nos 100 anos de comemoração da Revolução Russa, uma análise histórica da participação anarquista nos processos pré-revolução e durante os anos de Comunismo de Guerra. Em um segundo momento, a pesquisa busca descrever o principal movimento anarquista durante a Revolução Russa, ou seja, a atuação de Nestor Ivánovitch Makhno e seu Exército Negro na Ucrânia. Por fim, o estudo almeja fazer uma correlação dos fatos históricos vividos naquele período, pelos grupos de esquerda, com a atual conjuntura brasileira.

articles.title: “The seasons are the cornerstone of health”: The interconnection between local Aboriginal seasons and traditional foods on the wellbeing of Aboriginal peoples in the West Kimberley, Australia
articles.authors: theoni whyman, John Jacky, Janine McNamara, Belinda Neo, Brad Farrant, Eleanor Dunlop, Carrington Shepherd, Lucinda J Black
articles.abstract: Background: Traditional foods refer to native foods to Australia and have been consumed by Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples over millennia. Traditional Aboriginal diets are high in protein, fibre and essential vitamins and mineral and are low in sugar, salt and saturated fat. The health benefits associated with traditional food consumption have been documented over several decades. However, access to and consumption of these traditional foods has declined for many Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples across Australia. Objective: This study explored Aboriginal people’s stories and perspectives relating to traditional foods, and examined factors influencing their access and consumption among communities in the West Kimberley region of Australia. Methods: The project used a Community Based Participatory Research methodology and was conducted in partnership with two Aboriginal communities. A key group from each community provided governance, cultural guidance and feedback throughout the project. Qualitative interviews were conducted using yarning, a culturally appropriate Aboriginal form of communication. A total of 22 Aboriginal community members aged 30 years and over participated in an individual or group yarn discussing their knowledge and experiences of local traditional foods and food practices. Results: Two main themes were identified: (1) traditional foods as central to Social and Emotional Wellbeing; and (2) factors influencing the hunting, gathering, and consumption of traditional foods. Conclusions: This project highlights the beneficial impact of traditional foods and the local Aboriginal seasons on the holistic health of Aboriginal peoples. Policies and programs aimed at improving nutrition, wellbeing, and food security should incorporate and support access to traditional foods and related practices.

articles.title: Americans Favor Predistributive Over Redistributive Economic Policies
articles.authors: David Broska, Jonne Kamphorst, Robb Willer
articles.abstract: Redistributive policies designed to reduce economic inequality through taxes and transfers (e.g., safety-net benefits funded by progressive taxation) receive less support in the U.S. than in many Western democracies. Recent work suggests Americans may be more receptive to “predistributive” policies, which seek to reduce economic inequality before taxes and transfers (e.g., minimum-wage increases). Yet existing evidence leaves open whether this support gap characterizes contemporary Americans overall, and whether it persists when the same policy proposals are described in predistributive versus redistributive terms, with their core provisions held constant. Across 31 nationally representative U.S. survey waves fielded from 2015 to 2024 (total N=384,248), we find greater support for policies classified as predistributive than redistributive. We then fielded a preregistered experiment with a national U.S. sample (N=1,009), randomly varying whether otherwise identical policy proposals were described as increasing earnings for people lower in the income distribution, shifting resources from higher- to lower-income individuals, or presented only with the brief policy description given in all conditions. Support was higher under predistributive than redistributive descriptions. Comparison with the control condition clarifies this difference: predistributive descriptions received support comparable to control, whereas redistributive descriptions received lower support. Exploratory analyses suggest perceived respect for hard work and deservingness of beneficiaries were associated with the support gap. Together, these results suggest Americans prefer policies that reduce inequality by influencing earnings more than through taxes and transfers.

articles.title: Domiciliary care for people living with dementia: a systematic review of stakeholder perspectives
articles.authors: Ilianna Lourida, Elisabeth Grey, Katie Breheny, Rasa Mikelyte, Christopher Poyner
articles.abstract: Background: Most people living with dementia prefer to remain at home and as care needs increase, they often require domiciliary support. Demand for homecare services is growing internationally but quality varies widely, and what is understood as good quality care for people living with dementia at home remains unclear. Understanding perspectives of key stakeholders in homecare is essential to inform policy and practice. We conducted a systematic review to synthesise the perspectives of people living with dementia, their informal carers, homecare workers and managers to understand what constitutes good domiciliary care and the factors that enable or constrain its delivery. Methods: We followed a systematic review approach guided by recommendations for rapid review methods. Systematic searches of MEDLINE, PsycINFO and CINAHL (September 2024) were supplemented by reference list checking of included studies. Screening, data extraction and quality appraisal using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool were performed by one reviewer with partial verification by a second reviewer. Studies were prioritised for conceptual contribution and text richness and analysed using thematic synthesis. Results: Ninety-seven studies met the inclusion criteria and 31 were prioritised for synthesis. Stakeholders consistently described good care as individualised and relationship-centred, promoting agency and participation of people with dementia and reducing informal carer burden. Homecare experiences highlighted interpersonal and organisational factors required to deliver such care including dementia knowledge and collaboration with families, continuity of care, service flexibility and organisational support for workers. However, systemic influences showed how rigid commissioning, task-focused care, fragmented communication and workforce casualisation constrained best practice, limiting opportunities for relationship-building and personalised care. Conclusions: Good domiciliary care includes individualised, relationship-centred support for people living with dementia and their carers, yet everyday practice is complex and often constrained by interpersonal, organisational and systemic factors. Without organisational conditions that sustain skilled and consistent care alongside broader systemic change, homecare workers cannot deliver the care they aspire to and families expect. Addressing these influences is important for workforce stability and retention and for delivering care that aligns with principles of good dementia care.

articles.title: Weights for the German Longitudinal Environmental Study (GLEN)
articles.authors: Lisa Lessner, Christiane Bozoyan, Claudia	Schmiedeberg
articles.abstract: This report documents the construction and characteristics of the weights developed for the first five surveys of the German Longitudinal Environmental Study (GLEN). GLEN provides adjustment weights calibrated to 2024 data from official statistics (Microcensus and population update data). The report outlines the methodological decisions made in calculating the weights and describes their intended use for analyses based on the first GLEN Release.

articles.title: Men Request More Funding than Women in Grant Applications
articles.abstract: Science funding is the key to a sustainable innovation system. Yet, prior research documents systematic gender gap in funding amounts, which may emerge at the evaluation or application stage. Here, we study the latter mechanism by examining the funding amount requested by principal investigators (PI). Using a nationally representative sample from the Novo Nordisk Foundation covering 13,520 grant applications submitted between 2015 and 2022, we find that men PI request 25% more total budget than women PI. In addition, the gender gap increases over time and is greater among early-career PIs from the largest research area in medical sciences. This gender difference is robust to stringent controls for PI’s past experience, content novelty, linguistic features, and critical fixed-effect factors. Furthermore the gender gap persists when comparing applicants matched on observable confounding variables. Through mediation analysis, we identify three key mechanisms, including differences in presentation style, novelty preference, and research experience, which collectively explain 24% of the gender gap in funding requests. Importantly, we find that gender differences in requested amounts largely contribute to the gender gap in funded amounts. Our findings highlight the importance of understanding endogenous human behaviors in generating measurable gender inequality in funding outcomes, which provides a starting point for future work to dissect the origins of gender differences in formulating grant applications. We discuss practical interventions to mitigate the gender disparity in funding requests.

articles.title: The Geopolitical Displacement of Global Capital and the New Zealand Safe Haven Logic: A Critique of the Active Investor Plus (AIP) Visa Ecosystem
articles.authors: Yusuf Kamal, Taufeeq Rashid Pirzada, Amitabh Roy
articles.abstract: This academic commentary investigates the Geopolitical Displacement (GD) of international wealth and the emergence of New Zealand (NZ) as a preeminent safe haven for global capital. Driven by a rigorous Safe Haven Logic (SHL), High-Net-Worth Individuals (HNWI) are increasingly redirecting funds from volatile regions, specifically citing the United Arab Emirates regional fluctuations and Political or Social Instability in the United States, toward the New Zealand luxury real estate sector? The study deconstructs the Structural Liberalisation of the Active Investor Plus Visa, which lowered investment thresholds and introduced a strategic NZD 5 million residential property exemption, effectively creating an isolated ultra-luxury tier. The paper also Analyses the Trans-Tasman Vacuum (TTV) created by the abolition of the Australian Significant Investor Visa (SIV), positioning New Zealand as a primary indirect substitute for mobile wealth. Despite these economic gains, the commentary identifies an Ethical Paradox regarding the Minimum Physical Presence requirement of only 21 days, questioning whether the Programme facilitates genuine social integration or merely provides Residency Insurance (RI) for the global elite. The analysis concludes that while the Active Investor Plus ecosystem has successfully captured nearly NZD 4billion in committed funds, its long-term durability depends on balancing global capital attraction with national social integrity.

articles.title: Global fuel shocks as catalysts of sustainable travel behaviour change
articles.authors: Lauren Pearson, Ana Luiza Santos de Sa, Robyn Gerhard, Jamie Abrahams, Hossein Nosratzadeh, Toby Cumming, Ben Beck
articles.abstract: A sharp rise in fuel prices in March 2026 created a potential window for sustainable travel behaviour change in Australia. Yet who is positioned to change how they travel remains poorly understood. A population-representative online survey of 2,177 Australian adults, conducted in April 2026, captured pre-crisis travel behaviour, mode shift considerations, and conditions supporting sustained change. Explainable machine learning (XGBoost, SHAP, supervised k-means clustering) identified amenable subgroups for walking, cycling, e-bikes, and public transport. Three quarters of respondents had changed or considered changing their travel behaviour. Walking was the most commonly adopted new mode (20%), and travel avoidance the most common adaptation (41%). Financial hardship and younger age were dominant predictors of amenability. Supervised clustering revealed three recurrent archetypes - financially pressured young adults, employed urban professionals, and regional residents lacking service access. Realising equitable sustainable travel outcomes requires transport systems offering affordable, safe alternatives to all population groups.

articles.title: The Paradox of Innocence: An Empirical Phenomenological Analysis on Juvenile Delinquency from Non-Criminogenic Families
articles.abstract: This study examines pathways to juvenile delinquency among adolescents from non-criminogenic family backgrounds, addressing gaps in legal psychology that overemphasize familial criminality. Using a qualitative phenomenological design, semi-structured interviews were conducted with eight adolescents in a government rehabilitation facility. Data were analyzed using Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis, Colaizzi’s method, and systematic coding. Findings indicate that delinquency arises from interactions between developmental disruptions and socio-environmental influences, including familial disconnection, limited social integration, unstructured routines, and peer, media, and substance exposure. These factors intersect with emotional dysregulation, moral disengagement, and identity confusion. The study proposes an Exogenous-Endogenous Dynamic Model and an intervention framework.

articles.title: Presidential Rhetoric and Approval: A Preregistered Test Using Embedding-Based Construct Measurement
articles.abstract: Recent work has replicated Zullow and Seligman's (1990) finding that presidential-candidate optimism predicts election outcomes (Blyler et al., 2026). The present preregistered study examines a broader proposition: whether a nine-construct embedding-based framework of presidential rhetoric — spanning optimism, humility, affiliative humor, cooperation, pessimism, fear-mongering, defensiveness, aggression, and antagonism — predicts within-administration approval change. 470,995 speaker-turn chunks from U.S. presidential news conferences and press briefings, 1945–2025, were scored via cosine similarities between text-embedding-3-large vectors and per-construct centroids built from psychological-scale anchors; the anchor set was validated by two large language models (Cohen's κ .71–1.00). Preregistered multilevel models found no relationship between net rhetorical valence and subsequent approval at 7-, 14-, or 28- day lags; the null was robust to outlier trimming, leave-one-administration-out resampling, and a linearity check. The nine constructs collapse to one latent dimension at daily aggregation (r = −.98). Exploratory analyses surfaced a base-mobilization pattern: Antagonism positively and Optimism negatively predicted co-partisan approval. Within this multi-dimensional framework, presidential rhetoric does not appear to predict daily approval change.

articles.title: CONSCIOUS COST_A Necessary Condition for Sustainable Social Relations
articles.abstract: Abstract This paper presents findings from an autoethnographic investigation into the structural causes of relational breakdown, proposing "Conscious Cost" (Cs) — the degree to which an individual consciously recognizes the energy, time, and emotional burden invested in social relations — and advancing the following proposition: The recognition of Conscious Cost (Cs) is a necessary condition for sustainable social relations.
