Most relational breakdowns arise not from malice but from asymmetric cost recognition: one party invests significantly while failing to acknowledge that investment, producing a growing gap between actual cost (Cq) and recognized cost (Cs). This Gap = Cq − Cs — a structurally significant and under-recognized mechanism — generates resentment, burnout, and reciprocity collapse. The paper formalizes this mathematically; distinguishes Conscious Cost from Emotional Labor, the IKEA Effect, and the Just World Belief; and introduces the Reference Scale (R), the breadth of relational context against which individuals measure self-worth. Eight structural forces systematically shrink R, producing Cs distortion at scale. A four-quadrant framework (R × Cs) maps relational states and intervention pathways, grounded in an autoethnographic case study in which R expansion produced measurable shifts in Cs and reciprocity. Cs distortion is not individual failure but a structurally produced condition requiring structural design. Keywords: Conscious Cost; Qualitative Cost; Reference Scale; reciprocity; relational breakdown; emotional labor; autoethnography; self-worth independence; burnout; social relations AI Disclosure: The author used Claude (Anthropic) as a writing and research assistance tool in the preparation of this manuscript. All theoretical concepts, original insights, and intellectual contributions are the author's own.

articles.title: Methodological Trend in Child Social Work Research in the Age of AI: a Five-Year Review
articles.abstract: No study has systematically examined how data analysis methods have evolved in child and family social work research. This study analyzed 6,956 articles published between 2021 and 2025 in five SSCI-indexed journals (Child Abuse & Neglect, Child Maltreatment, Children and Youth Services Review, Family Relations, and Child and Adolescent Social Work Journal) to identify methodological trends coinciding with the emergence of generative artificial intelligence. Article abstracts were classified by study design and analytical approach using LLM-assisted content analysis, validated against human coding (Cohen's κ = .92 for study design, κ = .85 for analytical methods). Logistic regression models controlling for journal revealed significant increases in network analysis (OR = 1.851), machine learning (OR = 1.332), mediation analysis (OR = 1.075), and qualitative methods (OR = 1.078), alongside declines in bivariate analysis (OR = 0.954) and logistic regression (OR = 0.920). Pre- versus post-ChatGPT comparisons and sensitivity analyses confirmed these patterns. Findings document a shift toward more complex quantitative methods and growing methodological diversification, with implications for doctoral training, editorial standards, and the responsible use of AI in research.

articles.title: Coming into Being with Place
articles.abstract: Driven by the question, “what is the nature of being in place?”, the authors explore their personal experiences and how they came to understand place as more than an object or setting, but essential relations that shape the very rhythms of life, their personal identities, social rituals, and physical bodies. Using a narrative approach, the authors explore what is revealed about relationship between people and places when viewed through an Indigenous-inspired relational worldview rather than a Western materialist worldview. The authors introduce the Kithship Wheel, a visual lens that depicts three interlocking elements (person, people, and place) connected by four relational processes: valuing, being, knowing, and doing. The wheel is meant to help readers see how emotions, cultural narratives, and everyday practices co-constitute identity and belonging, offering a way to understand and deepen one’s sense of place beyond mainstream concepts in conventional sense of place research.

articles.title: A Historical Analysis of the Squatter Settlements (Sukumbasi) in Nepal and the Role of Smart Settlements
articles.abstract: The Sukumbasi question in Nepal must be understood as a historically layered problem of inequality, social displacement, legal ambiguity, and unstable state intervention, rather than a narrow or recent problem of informal settlement. Much of the policy discourse has treated Sukumbasi as an issue related to encroachment, unauthorized occupation, or land administration problem. Such a framing is analytically insufficient. The historical record suggests that the problem emerged through a lengthy process in which landlessness was first embedded within older systems of dependency, then made visible through the abolition of slavery, later managed through settlement and resettlement schemes, and eventually formalized through commissions, legal definitions, and statutory categories. Across successive political regimes, the Nepali state repeatedly acknowledged the problems of landlessness and created mechanisms to address it. Yet these interventions rarely matured into durable solutions. (De Schutter, 2021). The central argument of this historical review is that the Sukumbasi question in Nepal is not best explained by policy absence, but by the proliferation of public agencies without institutional continuity. The state has repeatedly identified the landless, classified them, collected applications for land allocation, and announced redistributive or regularizing measures. However, commissions have been short-lived, legal categories of Sukumbasi have shifted in scope, implementation has been interrupted, and institutional memory has repeatedly been fractured. Consequently, the history of the Sukumbasi issue is less a story of the invisibility of the settlers than one of repeated recognition followed by recurring suspension (De Schutter, 2021).

articles.title: Exploring the determinants of men’s involvement in group walking: A demonstrative network modelling study.
articles.authors: Brendan Smith, Eleanor Quested, Joanne A McVeigh, Matthew McDonald, Dominika Kwasnicka, Janelle Woods, Elizabeth Calleja, Hugh Riddell
articles.abstract: Background: Men in Australia are more likely than women to present with risk factors for coronary heart disease and are underrepresented in community-based physical activity (PA) initiatives. Understanding the characteristics of those who do participate can help identify groups not being reached. Traditional statistical approaches may not capture the complex interrelationships between demographic and behavioural factors that shape participation. This exploratory study demonstrates the utility of network modelling for public health research by identifying characteristics associated with men’s involvement in The National Heart Foundation Australia’s Heart Foundation Walking (HFW) groups program and examining how these characteristics interrelate. Methods: Exploratory network modelling was conducted using complete data from 30,909 participants (19.3% male; mean age=63.4, SD=14.38, range=18-96years). Variables included demographics (age, gender, socioeconomic status), location, enrolment year, role (organiser vs member) and self-reported PA at enrolment. Conditional dependencies among these variables were modelled to identify patterns associated with male participation. Results: Compared to women, men at enrolment were older (mean age=65 vs 63), self-reported meeting PA guidelines (44% vs 37%), and were more often organisers (2.3% vs 1.7%). Role was the most central variable, showing the strongest associations with others, while gender had lower centrality and was positioned peripherally. Self-report PA acted as a bridge node connecting characteristics. Conclusions: Network analysis visualised complex relationships among participant characteristics, revealing patterns not captured by traditional approaches. This method provides a tool for generating hypotheses about participation in community-based health initiatives. Further research should explore men’s experiences in HFW-groups to guide practical strategies for engagement.
